REGISTER: hpmcounter31
RULE 1: hpmcounter31 is a hardware performance‑counter CSR defined by the Zihpm extension and belongs to the hpmcounter3‑hpmcounter31 series.
RULE 2: When XLEN equals 64, hpmcounter31 is a 64‑bit unprivileged performance‑counter register.
RULE 3: When XLEN equals 32, the lower 32 bits of hpmcounter31 are accessed via the hpmcounter31 CSR and the upper 32 bits are accessed via the companion CSR hpmcounter31h.
RULE 4: hpmcounter31 is unprivileged and may be accessed in user mode, subject to any access control defined by privileged CSRs.
RULE 5: The presence of hpmcounter31 requires that both the Zihpm and Zicsr extensions be implemented.
RULE 6: The number of counters implemented, including hpmcounter31, and their bit‑width are platform‑specific.
RULE 7: If hpmcounter31 is unimplemented or ill‑configured, an access to hpmcounter31 may raise an illegal‑instruction exception or may return a constant value.
RULE 8: The events counted by hpmcounter31 are set by implementation‑specific mechanisms, typically via privileged CSRs.
RULE 9: Alternative execution environments may provide non‑standard methods to configure hpmcounter31.
RULE 10: The architecture does not provide a built‑in atomic snapshot latch for hpmcounter31, so software must use read‑before/after techniques to detect intervening context switches.
RULE 11: mhpmcounter31 is the hardware performance‑monitor event counter with index 31, the highest index among the counters mhpmcounter3 through mhpmcounter31.
RULE 12: mhpmcounter31 is a WARL (Write‑Any, Read‑Legal) CSR, so any written value is legal as defined by the WARL semantics.
RULE 13: mhpmcounter31 provides up to 64‑bit precision on both RV32 and RV64 implementations.
RULE 14: On an RV64 hart (XLEN = 64), a read of mhpmcounter31 returns the complete 64‑bit counter value.
RULE 15: On an RV64 hart, a write to mhpmcounter31 updates the entire 64‑bit register and the new value becomes visible after the writing instruction has otherwise completed.
RULE 16: On an RV32 hart (XLEN = 32), reading mhpmcounter31 returns bits 31‑0 of the underlying 64‑bit counter.
RULE 17: On an RV32 hart, writing mhpmcounter31 modifies only bits 31‑0 of the underlying 64‑bit counter.
RULE 18: The high 32 bits of the 64‑bit counter associated with mhpmcounter31 are accessed via the CSR mhpmcounter31h, which is present only when the Sscofpmf extension is implemented.
RULE 19: After a hart reset, mhpmcounter31 contains an arbitrary value and software may write any desired value to initialise it.
RULE 20: The incrementing event for mhpmcounter31 is selected by the CSR mhpmevent31, a 64‑bit WARL register where event code 0 indicates "no event".
RULE 21: If an implementation chooses not to provide mhpmcounter31, both mhpmcounter31 and its selector mhpmevent31 may be read‑only zero.
RULE 22: A conforming implementation is expected to implement mhpmcounter31, but may legally make mhpmcounter31 and its selector read‑only zero.
RULE 23: When siselect contains 0x5F, reads or writes of the CSR sireg access the low‑word of hpmcounter31.
RULE 24: When siselect contains 0x5F, reads or writes of the CSR sireg4 access the high‑word of hpmcounter31 (hpmcounter31h).
RULE 25: Access to hpmcounter31 via the indirect mechanism is permitted only if mcounteren[31] is set to 1.
RULE 26: Access to hpmcounter31 via the indirect mechanism from S‑mode is permitted only if menvcfg.CDE is set to 1.
RULE 27: If either mcounteren[31] is 0 or menvcfg.CDE is 0, any attempt to read or write hpmcounter31 through sireg while siselect = 0x5F raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 28: When menvcfg.CDE is 0, any attempt to access hpmcounter31 through sireg with siselect = 0x5F raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 29: When siselect = 0x41, any attempt to access hpmcounter31 via sireg raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 30: If the architectural extension required for hpmcounter31 is not implemented, any attempt to access hpmcounter31 via sireg with siselect = 0x5F raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 31: While executing in M‑mode or S‑mode with siselect = 0x5F, the delegation, enable‑bit, and extension requirements govern all accesses to hpmcounter31.
RULE 32: Accesses to hpmcounter31 via sireg from VS‑mode or VU‑mode cause a virtual‑instruction exception when the hypervisor extension is present, otherwise they cause an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 33: The low‑word CSR sireg that maps to hpmcounter31 is always accessible regardless of the processor’s XLEN setting.
RULE 34: When XLEN = 64, any attempt to access the high‑word alias sireg4 (or sireg5) of hpmcounter31 raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 35: In M-mode, accessing hpmcounter31 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xc1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 36: In S-mode, accessing hpmcounter31 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xc1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 37: In U-mode, accessing hpmcounter31 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xc1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 38: In M-mode, accessing hpmcounter31 register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0xc1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 39: In M-mode, accessing hpmcounter31 register while Sscofpmf extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Sscofpmf; csrr a0, 0xc1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 40: In M-mode, accessing hpmcounter31 register while Zicsr extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicsr; csrr a0, 0xc1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 41: In M-mode, accessing hpmcounter31 register while Zihpm extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zihpm; csrr a0, 0xc1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: jvt
RULE 1: The Zcmt extension adds a control and status register named jvt.
RULE 2: When the Smstateen extension is implemented, the jvt CSR is only usable if its associated state‑enable bit is set.
RULE 3: The architectural definition of the jvt CSR is documented together with the table jump base vector and control register.
RULE 4: The jvt CSR holds the base address of the jump‑vector table, and the table’s base address is stored in the jvt register.
RULE 5: Each entry in the jump‑vector table referenced by the jvt register is XLEN bits wide.
RULE 6: For any function that may be invoked both with linking (as a normal call) and without linking (as a tail‑call), the jump‑vector table accessed via the jvt register must contain two distinct entries for that function.
RULE 7: The jvt CSR is located at address 0x017 and has URW (user‑read/write) access permission.
RULE 8: The jvt CSR is an XLEN‑bit WARL (write‑anything‑read‑legal) read/write register.
RULE 9: In the jvt CSR, bits 5‑0 form the mode field, which is a WARL field.
RULE 10: In the jvt CSR, bits XLEN‑1 down to 6 form the base field, which is a WARL field.
RULE 11: The full address jvt.base is formed by concatenating the stored base field bits with six zero bits, making the low six bits of jvt.base implicitly zero.
RULE 12: The value stored in the jvt base field must ensure that the resulting jvt.base address is 64‑byte aligned.
RULE 13: When virtual memory is enabled, the jvt.base address is interpreted as a virtual address.
RULE 14: The memory region beginning at jvt.base is treated as instruction memory for table‑jump instructions and must be executable.
RULE 15: The jvt.mode value 0b000000 indicates jump‑table mode, which all implementations must support.
RULE 16: Any jvt.mode value other than 0b000000 is reserved for future standard use.
RULE 17: The jvt mode field is WARL, so only mode values implemented by a particular implementation may be written and retained, and software can discover supported modes by writing and reading back the field.
RULE 18: If the optional jump‑table instruction set is implemented, the jvt CSR must be present but may be read‑only.
RULE 19: When the jvt CSR is writable, the set of admissible jvt.base and jvt.mode values may differ by implementation, provided they obey the alignment and WARL constraints.
RULE 20: The jvt CSR constitutes architectural state belonging to the software context and must be saved and restored on context switches.
RULE 21: If the Smstateen extension is implemented, bit 2 of each of the mstateen0, sstateen0, and hstateen0 CSRs controls access to the jvt CSR.
RULE 22: When bit 2 of the relevant stateen0 CSR is zero, any lower‑privilege attempt to read or write the jvt CSR or to execute a cm.jalt or cm.jt instruction causes an illegal‑instruction trap (or a virtual‑instruction trap if appropriate).
RULE 23: In implementations where the jvt CSR is read‑only, the register must still exist and contain a valid (possibly implementation‑defined) value, but software cannot modify it.
RULE 24: If jvt.mode = 0, the cm.jt instruction behaves as defined in this specification.
RULE 25: If jvt.mode holds a reserved value, the encoding of cm.jt is undefined and the instruction is illegal.
RULE 26: Future defined values of jvt.mode may alter the behavior of cm.jt.
RULE 27: The jvt.base field supplies the base address of the jump‑vector table used by cm.jt.
RULE 28: For XLEN = 32, the jump‑table entry address is computed as jvt.base plus (index << 2).
RULE 29: For XLEN = 64, the jump‑table entry address is computed as jvt.base plus (index << 3).
RULE 30: The memory word fetched from the address calculated using jvt.base provides the target address for the cm.jt jump.
RULE 31: The jvt register contains at least a mode field that is referenced by the cm.jalt instruction.
RULE 32: The jvt register contains at least a base field that is referenced by the cm.jalt instruction.
RULE 33: When jvt.mode = 0, the cm.jalt instruction behaves as defined in this specification.
RULE 34: If jvt.mode holds a reserved value, the cm.jalt instruction is reserved and its behavior is architecturally undefined.
RULE 35: Future defined values of jvt.mode may alter the behavior of the cm.jalt instruction.
RULE 36: The jvt.base field provides the starting address of the jump vector table used by cm.jalt.
RULE 37: For a 32‑bit XLEN, the table address used by cm.jalt is computed as jvt.base + (index << 2).
RULE 38: For a 64‑bit XLEN, the table address used by cm.jalt is computed as jvt.base + (index << 3).
RULE 39: The processor fetches the target address from memory at the table address derived from jvt.base and then jumps to that address while linking the return address into ra.
RULE 40: The jvt CSR is provided by the Zcmt extension.
RULE 41: The jvt bit controls access to the jvt CSR.
RULE 42: In M-mode, accessing jvt register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x017; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 43: In S-mode, accessing jvt register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x017; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 44: In U-mode, accessing jvt register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x017; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 45: In M-mode, setting jvt.BASE (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x017, a0; csrr a1, 0x017; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 46: In M-mode, setting jvt.MODE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x017, a0; csrr a1, 0x017; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 47: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the jvt register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x017, a0; csrr a1, 0x017; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 48: In M-mode, accessing jvt register while Smstateen extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smstateen; csrr a0, 0x017; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 49: In M-mode, accessing jvt register while Zcmt extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zcmt; csrr a0, 0x017; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mcause
RULE 1: When an unexpected trap occurs while the Smrnmi extension is implemented and mnstatus.NMIE = 1, the value that would have been written to mcause for that trap is instead written to the architectural register mncause.
RULE 2: The only normative specification of mcause behavior in the supplied description is that its intended trap value is transferred to mncause when delivering the trap to the RNMI handler.
RULE 3: Each synchronous exception has a dedicated bit in the medeleg register whose position equals the numeric cause code value stored in the mcause register.
RULE 4: The mcause register is written with the trap cause only when the trap is taken in machine mode.
RULE 5: When a trap is delegated to S‑mode, the mcause register is not written.
RULE 6: For an interrupt, the mcause register reports a cause number i that corresponds to bit i in both the mip and mie registers.
RULE 7: mcause is a MXLEN‑bit read‑write register.
RULE 8: When a trap is taken into M‑mode, the implementation writes mcause with a code that identifies the event that caused the trap.
RULE 9: If a trap is not taken, the implementation never writes mcause.
RULE 10: Software may explicitly write mcause at any time.
RULE 11: The most‑significant bit of mcause is the Interrupt bit.
RULE 12: The Interrupt bit of mcause is set to 1 when the trap was caused by an interrupt and set to 0 when the trap was caused by an exception.
RULE 13: The remaining bits of mcause after the most‑significant bit form the Exception Code field.
RULE 14: The Exception Code field of mcause contains the code identifying the last exception or interrupt.
RULE 15: The Exception Code field of mcause is a WLRL field, therefore only codes defined by the implementation are guaranteed to be representable.
RULE 16: When multiple synchronous exceptions are possible for an instruction, the implementation selects the exception with the highest priority and writes its code into the Exception Code field of mcause.
RULE 17: The priority order for standard synchronous exceptions that determines which code is placed in mcause is defined by the architectural specification.
RULE 18: The priority of any custom synchronous exceptions that appear in mcause is implementation‑defined.
RULE 19: Because the Interrupt bit is the sign bit of mcause, a branch on the sign of mcause distinguishes interrupts from other traps.
RULE 20: Shifting mcause left removes the Interrupt bit and scales the Exception Code so it can be used as an index into a trap‑vector table.
RULE 21: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=0, the code is reserved for future use.
RULE 22: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=1, the code indicates a supervisor software interrupt.
RULE 23: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=3, the code indicates a machine software interrupt.
RULE 24: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=5, the code indicates a supervisor timer interrupt.
RULE 25: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=7, the code indicates a machine timer interrupt.
RULE 26: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=9, the code indicates a supervisor external interrupt.
RULE 27: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=11, the code indicates a machine external interrupt.
RULE 28: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=13, the code indicates a counter‑overflow interrupt.
RULE 29: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code=14 or 15, the codes are reserved.
RULE 30: When mcause has Interrupt=1 and Exception Code is 16 or greater, the codes are designated for platform use.
RULE 31: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=0, the code indicates an instruction address misaligned exception.
RULE 32: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=1, the code indicates an instruction access fault exception.
RULE 33: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=2, the code indicates an illegal instruction exception.
RULE 34: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=3, the code indicates a breakpoint exception.
RULE 35: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=4, the code indicates a load address misaligned exception.
RULE 36: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=5, the code indicates a load access fault exception.
RULE 37: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=6, the code indicates a store/AMO address misaligned exception.
RULE 38: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=7, the code indicates a store/AMO access fault exception.
RULE 39: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=8, the code indicates an environment call from U‑mode exception.
RULE 40: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=9, the code indicates an environment call from S‑mode exception.
RULE 41: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=11, the code indicates an environment call from M‑mode exception.
RULE 42: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=12, the code indicates an instruction page fault exception.
RULE 43: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=13, the code indicates a load page fault exception.
RULE 44: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=15, the code indicates a store/AMO page fault exception.
RULE 45: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=16, the code indicates a double trap exception.
RULE 46: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=18, the code indicates a software check exception.
RULE 47: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=19, the code indicates a hardware error exception.
RULE 48: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code=14, 17, or 20‑23, the codes are reserved.
RULE 49: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code is in the range 24‑31, the codes are designated for custom use.
RULE 50: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code is in the range 32‑47, the codes are reserved.
RULE 51: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code is in the range 48‑63, the codes are designated for custom use.
RULE 52: When mcause has Interrupt=0 and Exception Code is 64 or greater, the codes are reserved.
RULE 53: Load and load‑reserved instructions raise a load‑related exception code that is written into mcause when they cause a trap.
RULE 54: Store, store‑conditional, and AMO instructions raise a store/AMO‑related exception code that is written into mcause when they cause a trap.
RULE 55: Upon reset, the mcause register is written with a value that indicates the cause of the reset.
RULE 56: The values placed in mcause after reset are implementation‑specific.
RULE 57: An implementation that does not differentiate among reset causes must set mcause = 0.
RULE 58: An implementation that differentiates reset conditions must use the value 0 in mcause only to represent the most complete reset condition.
RULE 59: All other reset causes must be encoded in mcause with non‑zero values.
RULE 60: Reset‑time mcause values may be the same as values used for synchronous exceptions.
RULE 61: The specification requires that any overlap of mcause values between reset causes and synchronous exceptions cause no ambiguity because the program counter is typically set to a different address on reset than on synchronous exception traps.
RULE 62: When a non‑maskable interrupt occurs, the mcause register is written with a value that indicates the source of the NMI.
RULE 63: The values written to mcause for a non‑maskable interrupt are defined by the implementation.
RULE 64: The high interrupt bit of mcause shall be set to 1 to indicate that the trap was an interrupt.
RULE 65: The exception‑code field value 0 in mcause is reserved to represent an unknown cause.
RULE 66: Implementations that do not differentiate NMI sources via mcause shall write 0 in the exception‑code field of mcause.
RULE 67: When an unresumable non‑maskable interrupt (UNMI) occurs, the processor jumps to a handler in machine mode and overwrites the current mcause register value.
RULE 68: Because the UNMI overwrites mcause, the previous mcause value is not recoverable, making execution non‑resumable after such an interrupt.
RULE 69: In a double‑trap exception caused by the S‑mode‑disable‑trap bit already being set, the hardware automatically sets mcause to the constant value 16, which identifies a double‑trap exception.
RULE 70: During a double‑trap exception the hardware writes all standard trap registers with the same values they would have received for the unexpected trap, but it does not write mcause (nor mtval2) with those values.
RULE 71: When a double‑trap exception is delivered to M‑mode, the value that would have been written to mcause by the unexpected trap is instead written to mtval2.
RULE 72: Software must not write mcause when a double‑trap exception is taken; the hardware alone sets mcause.
RULE 73: The hypervisor extension adds additional interrupt and exception codes to the machine‑mode cause register mcause.
RULE 74: The same set of cause codes defined for mcause also applies to the supervisor‑mode cause register scause.
RULE 75: In mcause the most‑significant bit set to 1 indicates that the value encodes an interrupt.
RULE 76: In mcause the most‑significant bit cleared to 0 indicates that the value encodes an exception.
RULE 77: An interrupt code of 1 in mcause is Reserved.
RULE 78: An interrupt code of 1 in mcause also indicates a Supervisor software interrupt.
RULE 79: An interrupt code of 2 in mcause indicates a Virtual supervisor software interrupt.
RULE 80: An interrupt code of 3 in mcause indicates a Machine software interrupt.
RULE 81: An interrupt code of 4 in mcause is Reserved.
RULE 82: An interrupt code of 5 in mcause indicates a Supervisor timer interrupt.
RULE 83: An interrupt code of 6 in mcause indicates a Virtual supervisor timer interrupt.
RULE 84: An interrupt code of 7 in mcause indicates a Machine timer interrupt.
RULE 85: An interrupt code of 8 in mcause is Reserved.
RULE 86: An interrupt code of 9 in mcause indicates a Supervisor external interrupt.
RULE 87: An interrupt code of 10 in mcause indicates a Virtual supervisor external interrupt.
RULE 88: An interrupt code of 11 in mcause indicates a Machine external interrupt.
RULE 89: An interrupt code of 12 in mcause indicates a Supervisor guest external interrupt.
RULE 90: An interrupt code of 13 in mcause is Reserved for counter‑overflow interrupt.
RULE 91: Interrupt codes 14 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 92: Interrupt codes 15 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 93: Interrupt codes greater than or equal to 16 in mcause are Designated for platform use.
RULE 94: An exception code of 0 in mcause indicates Instruction address misaligned.
RULE 95: An exception code of 1 in mcause indicates Instruction access fault.
RULE 96: An exception code of 2 in mcause indicates Illegal instruction.
RULE 97: An exception code of 3 in mcause indicates Breakpoint.
RULE 98: An exception code of 4 in mcause indicates Load address misaligned.
RULE 99: An exception code of 5 in mcause indicates Load access fault.
RULE 100: An exception code of 6 in mcause indicates Store/AMO address misaligned.
RULE 101: An exception code of 7 in mcause indicates Store/AMO access fault.
RULE 102: An exception code of 8 in mcause indicates Environment call from U‑mode or VU‑mode.
RULE 103: An exception code of 9 in mcause indicates Environment call from HS‑mode.
RULE 104: An exception code of 10 in mcause indicates Environment call from VS‑mode.
RULE 105: An exception code of 11 in mcause indicates Environment call from M‑mode.
RULE 106: An exception code of 12 in mcause indicates Instruction page fault.
RULE 107: An exception code of 13 in mcause indicates Load page fault.
RULE 108: An exception code of 14 in mcause is Reserved.
RULE 109: An exception code of 15 in mcause indicates Store/AMO page fault.
RULE 110: Exception codes 16 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 111: Exception codes 17 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 112: Exception codes 18 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 113: Exception codes 19 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 114: An exception code of 20 in mcause indicates Instruction guest‑page fault.
RULE 115: An exception code of 21 in mcause indicates Load guest‑page fault.
RULE 116: An exception code of 22 in mcause indicates Virtual instruction.
RULE 117: An exception code of 23 in mcause indicates Store/AMO guest‑page fault.
RULE 118: Exception codes 24 through 31 in mcause are Designated for custom use.
RULE 119: Exception codes 32 through 47 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 120: Exception codes 48 through 63 in mcause are Designated for custom use.
RULE 121: Exception codes greater than or equal to 64 in mcause are Reserved.
RULE 122: An mcause value of 10 indicates an ECALL from VS‑mode.
RULE 123: An mcause value of 9 indicates an ECALL from HS‑mode or S‑mode.
RULE 124: The distinct mcause values for ECALL allow the traps to be delegated separately.
RULE 125: When the hypervisor extension is present and the V bit is 1, privileged or hypervisor instructions that would otherwise raise an illegal‑instruction exception raise a virtual‑instruction exception identified by mcause code 22.
RULE 126: On a virtual‑instruction trap the mtval register (or stval for supervisor traps) is written in the same way as for an illegal‑instruction trap.
RULE 127: The tables presented constitute the complete normative list of values that mcause may contain when the hypervisor extension is implemented.
RULE 128: When a trap is taken into M‑mode, the hardware must write the mcause CSR as part of the trap entry.
RULE 129: The M‑mode trap‑entry actions require the hardware to write the mcause CSR together with the mepc, mtval, mtval2, and mtinst CSRs.
RULE 130: In M-mode, accessing mcause register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x342; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 131: In S-mode, accessing mcause register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x342; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 132: In U-mode, accessing mcause register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x342; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 133: In M-mode, setting mcause.INT (bit 63) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000000000; csrs 0x342, a0; csrr a1, 0x342; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000].
RULE 134: In M-mode, setting mcause.CODE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x342, a0; csrr a1, 0x342; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 135: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mcause register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x342, a0; csrr a1, 0x342; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 136: In M-mode, accessing mcause register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x342; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 137: In M-mode, accessing mcause register while Smrnmi extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smrnmi; csrr a0, 0x342; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mcounteren
RULE 1: mcounteren is a 32‑bit register.
RULE 2: mcounteren controls accessibility of hardware performance‑monitoring counters to the next‑lower privileged mode but does not affect the counters’ counting behavior.
RULE 3: When the CY bit of mcounteren is 0, any read of the cycle or cycleh CSR in S‑mode or U‑mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 4: When the CY bit of mcounteren is 1, reads of the cycle or cycleh CSR are permitted in the next‑implemented lower privilege mode (S‑mode if present, otherwise U‑mode).
RULE 5: When the TM bit of mcounteren is 0, any read of the time or timeh CSR in S‑mode or U‑mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 6: When the TM bit of mcounteren is 1, reads of the time or timeh CSR are permitted in the next‑implemented lower privilege mode.
RULE 7: When the IR bit of mcounteren is 0, any read of the instret or instreth CSR in S‑mode or U‑mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 8: When the IR bit of mcounteren is 1, reads of the instret or instreth CSR are permitted in the next‑implemented lower privilege mode.
RULE 9: When an HPM‑n bit of mcounteren is 0, any read of the hpmcounter n or hpmcounter n h CSR in S‑mode or U‑mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 10: When an HPM‑n bit of mcounteren is 1, reads of the hpmcounter n or hpmcounter n h CSR are permitted in the next‑implemented lower privilege mode.
RULE 11: The term "next‑implemented lower privilege mode" used by mcounteren means S‑mode if the implementation provides it; otherwise it means U‑mode.
RULE 12: The mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR cycle that mirrors the machine‑mode CSR mcycle.
RULE 13: The mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR cycleh (when XLEN=32) that mirrors the machine‑mode CSR mcycleh.
RULE 14: The mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR instret that mirrors the machine‑mode CSR minstret.
RULE 15: The mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR instrreth (when XLEN=32) that mirrors the machine‑mode CSR minstreth.
RULE 16: The mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR hpmcounter n that mirrors the machine‑mode CSR mhpmcounter n.
RULE 17: The mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR hpmcounter n h (when XLEN=32) that mirrors the machine‑mode CSR mhpmcounter n h.
RULE 18: The mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR time that reflects the memory‑mapped mtime register.
RULE 19: When XLEN=32, the mcounteren register defines the read‑only shadow CSR timeh that reflects the upper 32 bits of the memory‑mapped mtime register, while time reflects the lower 32 bits.
RULE 20: An implementation may realize reads of time and timeh defined by mcounteren as actual loads from the memory‑mapped mtime register or may emulate the functionality in machine mode for lower‑privilege accesses.
RULE 21: Any hart that implements U‑mode must provide the mcounteren register.
RULE 22: All fields of the mcounteren register are WARL (write‑any‑read‑legal) and may be read‑only zero.
RULE 23: A zero value in a field of the mcounteren register causes the corresponding counter reads in less‑privileged modes to raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 24: Any hart that does not implement U‑mode shall not provide the mcounteren register.
RULE 25: The mcounteren CSR contains a delegation bit for each performance‑monitoring counter that determines whether S‑mode may access that counter’s state.
RULE 26: The mcounteren register enables M‑mode to grant S‑mode read access to the counters whose delegation bits are set.
RULE 27: When the Smcdeleg/Ssccfg extension is implemented and the menvcfg.CDE bit is 1, the delegation bits in mcounteren are used to delegate the corresponding counters to S‑mode.
RULE 28: A counter i is considered delegated to S‑mode only if both mcounteren[i] equals 1 and menvcfg.CDE equals 1.
RULE 29: If counter i is delegated (i.e., mcounteren[i] = 1 and menvcfg.CDE = 1), the state of counter i may be read or written via a sireg* CSR when siselect holds the value 0x40 plus i.
RULE 30: An illegal‑instruction exception is generated when M‑mode or S‑mode attempts to access a sireg* CSR while siselect selects a counter whose mcounteren bit is 0.
RULE 31: An illegal‑instruction exception is also generated when an attempt is made to access a sireg* CSR while siselect equals 0x41, independent of other conditions on mcounteren.
RULE 32: Setting a bit in mcounteren does not affect whether the corresponding bit in scounteren is writable.
RULE 33: User mode may only access a hardware performance counter if the corresponding bits in both scounteren and mcounteren are set.
RULE 34: The mcounteren register includes a TM (timer‑mask) bit.
RULE 35: When the TM bit in mcounteren is zero, any attempt to read or write the stimecmp register while executing in S‑mode, U‑mode, VS‑mode, or VU‑mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 36: When the TM bit in mcounteren is zero, any attempt to read or write the vstimecmp register while executing in S‑mode, U‑mode, VS‑mode, or VU‑mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 37: When the TM bit in mcounteren is one, access to stimecmp or vstimecmp is permitted in S‑mode provided the implementation includes the accessed register(s).
RULE 38: When the TM bit in mcounteren is one, the implementation includes the vstimecmp register, and the TM bit in hcounteren does not otherwise prevent the access, then access to vstimecmp via the stimecmp address is permitted in VS‑mode.
RULE 39: The TM bit in mcounteren, together with the TM bit in hcounteren, determines whether VS‑mode code can access the virtual timer compare register vstimecmp (accessed via stimecmp).
RULE 40: When the TM bit in hcounteren is clear (0) and the TM bit in mcounteren is set (1), any VS‑mode attempt to access vstimecmp causes a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 41: When both the TM bit in hcounteren and the TM bit in mcounteren are set (1), access to vstimecmp is permitted in VS‑mode provided the implementation includes that register.
RULE 42: The mcounteren CSR provides a separate enable bit for each hardware performance‑monitor counter that determines whether the corresponding scountovf bit is readable in S‑mode.
RULE 43: In S‑mode (and HS‑mode), a scountovf bit X is readable only if the mcounteren bit X is set; if the mcounteren bit X is clear, a read of that scountovf bit returns zero.
RULE 44: In VS‑mode, a scountovf bit X is readable only if both the mcounteren bit X and the hcounteren bit X are set; otherwise a read of that scountovf bit returns zero.
RULE 45: The mcounteren machine‑mode register contains a separate enable bit for each performance‑monitoring counter that can be accessed from a virtualized execution environment (V=1), specifically a CY bit for the cycle counter, a TM bit for the time counter, an IR bit for the instret counter, and an HPM n bit for each hardware performance‑monitoring counter hpmcounter n where n ranges from 0 to 31.
RULE 46: When the processor is in virtualized mode (V=1), if the corresponding bit in hcounteren is clear (0) and the same bit in mcounteren is set (1), any attempt to read the associated counter must cause a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 47: When V=1, if the corresponding bit in hcounteren is set (1), access to the counter is permitted regardless of the value of the corresponding bit in mcounteren, unless another architectural rule forbids it.
RULE 48: The mcounteren register therefore acts as a higher‑privilege gate, whereby a set bit enables a machine‑mode exception only when the lower‑privilege hcounteren bit does not also enable the counter.
RULE 49: The mcounteren CSR controls the visibility of machine‑mode counter CSRs to lower‑privilege virtualized execution contexts.
RULE 50: When a virtual instruction attempts to access a counter CSR, the corresponding bits in mcounteren are consulted together with the corresponding bits in hcounteren and scounteren to determine permission.
RULE 51: In VS‑mode, if a non‑high‑half counter CSR is accessed and the corresponding hcounteren bit is 0 while the same bit in mcounteren is 1, a virtual‑instruction exception with exception code 22 is raised.
RULE 52: In VS‑mode on an XLEN‑32 implementation, if a high‑half counter CSR is accessed and the corresponding hcounteren bit is 0 while the same bit in mcounteren is 1, a virtual‑instruction exception with exception code 22 is raised.
RULE 53: In VU‑mode, if a non‑high‑half counter CSR is accessed and either the corresponding hcounteren bit or the corresponding scounteren bit is 0 while the same bit in mcounteren is 1, a virtual‑instruction exception with exception code 22 is raised.
RULE 54: In VU‑mode on an XLEN‑32 implementation, if a high‑half counter CSR is accessed and either the corresponding hcounteren bit or the corresponding scounteren bit is 0 while the same bit in mcounteren is 1, a virtual‑instruction exception with exception code 22 is raised.
RULE 55: A bit set to 1 in mcounteren does not by itself grant access to a counter CSR; access also requires the corresponding enable bit to be 1 in the lower‑privilege enable registers (hcounteren for hypervisor level, scounteren for supervisor level).
RULE 56: When the lower‑level enable bit is 0 and the corresponding mcounteren bit is 1, the attempted access is blocked and the processor raises a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 57: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 58: In S-mode, accessing mcounteren register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 59: In U-mode, accessing mcounteren register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 60: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM31 (bit 31) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000000].
RULE 61: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM30 (bit 30) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000000].
RULE 62: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM29 (bit 29) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20000000].
RULE 63: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM28 (bit 28) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000000].
RULE 64: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM27 (bit 27) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000].
RULE 65: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM26 (bit 26) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000000].
RULE 66: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM25 (bit 25) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000000].
RULE 67: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM24 (bit 24) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000].
RULE 68: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM23 (bit 23) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800000].
RULE 69: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM22 (bit 22) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400000].
RULE 70: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM21 (bit 21) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200000].
RULE 71: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM20 (bit 20) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000].
RULE 72: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM19 (bit 19) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000].
RULE 73: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM18 (bit 18) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000].
RULE 74: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM17 (bit 17) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20000].
RULE 75: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM16 (bit 16) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000].
RULE 76: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM15 (bit 15) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000].
RULE 77: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM14 (bit 14) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000].
RULE 78: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM13 (bit 13) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000].
RULE 79: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM12 (bit 12) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000].
RULE 80: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM11 (bit 11) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800].
RULE 81: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM10 (bit 10) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400].
RULE 82: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM9 (bit 9) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200].
RULE 83: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM8 (bit 8) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100].
RULE 84: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM7 (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 85: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM6 (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 86: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM5 (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 87: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM4 (bit 4) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10].
RULE 88: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.HPM3 (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 89: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.IR (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 90: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.TM (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 91: In M-mode, setting mcounteren.CY (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 92: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mcounteren register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x306, a0; csrr a1, 0x306; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 93: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 94: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register while S extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: S; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 95: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register while Smcdeleg extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smcdeleg; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 96: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register while Ssccfg extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Ssccfg; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 97: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register while U extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: U; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 98: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register while Zicntr extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicntr; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 99: In M-mode, accessing mcounteren register while Zihpm extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zihpm; csrr a0, 0x306; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mcountinhibit
RULE 1: mcountinhibit is a 32‑bit WARL CSR.
RULE 2: mcountinhibit controls whether hardware performance‑monitoring counters increment, without affecting their accessibility.
RULE 3: The CY field of mcountinhibit controls the incrementing of the mcycle counter.
RULE 4: The IR field of mcountinhibit controls the incrementing of the minstret counter.
RULE 5: Each HPM_n bit of mcountinhibit controls the incrementing of the corresponding mhpmcounter_n counter.
RULE 6: When a CY, IR, or HPM_n bit in mcountinhibit is clear (0), the associated counter increments as defined by the architecture.
RULE 7: When a CY, IR, or HPM_n bit in mcountinhibit is set (1), the associated counter does not increment.
RULE 8: If the mcycle CSR is shared between harts on the same core, the CY field of mcountinhibit is also shared and a write to it is visible to all harts sharing that mcycle CSR.
RULE 9: If an implementation does not provide the mcountinhibit CSR, the implementation must behave as if mcountinhibit were present and hard‑wired to zero, enabling all counters.
RULE 10: The mtime counter cannot be inhibited using the mcountinhibit mechanism.
RULE 11: The mcountinhibit register is allocated to CSR address 0x320.
RULE 12: The mcountinhibit CSR has a supervisor‑mode alias named scountinhibit whose visible bits are masked according to counter delegation.
RULE 13: For any counter that is delegated to supervisor mode, the corresponding bit(s) in mcountinhibit are directly readable and writable via the scountinhibit CSR.
RULE 14: For any counter that is not delegated to supervisor mode, the corresponding bit(s) in mcountinhibit read as zero when accessed through scountinhibit and are read‑only via scountinhibit.
RULE 15: Writes to scountinhibit that target bits corresponding to counters not delegated to supervisor mode have no effect on the corresponding bits in mcountinhibit.
RULE 16: In M-mode, accessing mcountinhibit register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x320; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 17: In S-mode, accessing mcountinhibit register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x320; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 18: In U-mode, accessing mcountinhibit register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x320; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 19: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM31 (bit 31) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000000].
RULE 20: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM30 (bit 30) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000000].
RULE 21: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM29 (bit 29) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20000000].
RULE 22: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM28 (bit 28) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000000].
RULE 23: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM27 (bit 27) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000].
RULE 24: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM26 (bit 26) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000000].
RULE 25: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM25 (bit 25) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000000].
RULE 26: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM24 (bit 24) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000].
RULE 27: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM23 (bit 23) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800000].
RULE 28: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM22 (bit 22) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400000].
RULE 29: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM21 (bit 21) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200000].
RULE 30: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM20 (bit 20) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000].
RULE 31: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM19 (bit 19) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000].
RULE 32: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM18 (bit 18) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000].
RULE 33: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM17 (bit 17) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20000].
RULE 34: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM16 (bit 16) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000].
RULE 35: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM15 (bit 15) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000].
RULE 36: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM14 (bit 14) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000].
RULE 37: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM13 (bit 13) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000].
RULE 38: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM12 (bit 12) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000].
RULE 39: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM11 (bit 11) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800].
RULE 40: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM10 (bit 10) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400].
RULE 41: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM9 (bit 9) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200].
RULE 42: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM8 (bit 8) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100].
RULE 43: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM7 (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 44: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM6 (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 45: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM5 (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 46: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM4 (bit 4) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10].
RULE 47: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.HPM3 (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 48: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.IR (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 49: In M-mode, setting mcountinhibit.CY (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 50: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mcountinhibit register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x320, a0; csrr a1, 0x320; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 51: In M-mode, accessing mcountinhibit register while Zicntr extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicntr; csrr a0, 0x320; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 52: In M-mode, accessing mcountinhibit register while Zihpm extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zihpm; csrr a0, 0x320; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mcycle
RULE 1: mcycle is defined as a Control and Status Register (CSR).
RULE 2: mcycle is classified as a visible CSR, meaning it is visible to other harts and devices.
RULE 3: Because mcycle is a visible CSR, all CSR‑ordering constraints that apply to visible CSRs also apply to mcycle.
RULE 4: An explicit CSR read of mcycle is classified as a device input (I) operation.
RULE 5: An explicit CSR read of mcycle returns the value of mcycle that existed before the read instruction is executed.
RULE 6: An explicit CSR write to mcycle is classified as a device output (O) operation.
RULE 7: An explicit CSR write to mcycle suppresses and overrides any implicit writes or modifications to mcycle performed by the same instruction.
RULE 8: A CSR access to mcycle is performed after any prior instruction that modifies or is modified by the mcycle state, unless a fence or other explicit reordering mechanism intervenes.
RULE 9: A CSR access to mcycle is performed before any subsequent instruction that modifies or is modified by the mcycle state, unless a fence or other explicit reordering mechanism intervenes.
RULE 10: The full consequences of a CSR side effect on mcycle are observable by the immediately following instruction in program order.
RULE 11: No preceding instruction can observe the effect of a CSR side effect on mcycle out of order.
RULE 12: The mcycle CSR counts the number of clock cycles executed by the processor core on which the hart is running.
RULE 13: The mcycle CSR provides 64‑bit precision on all RV32 and RV64 harts.
RULE 14: After a hart reset, the mcycle register contains an arbitrary value that software may overwrite with any desired value.
RULE 15: A write to the mcycle CSR takes effect only after the writing instruction has otherwise completed.
RULE 16: When the mcycle CSR is shared between harts on the same core, a write to mcycle becomes visible to all harts that share that CSR.
RULE 17: Implementations must provide a mechanism for software to determine which harts share a given mcycle CSR.
RULE 18: On an XLEN=32 hart, a read of the mcycle CSR returns bits 31‑0 of the 64‑bit cycle counter.
RULE 19: On an XLEN=32 hart, a write to the mcycle CSR modifies only bits 31‑0 of the 64‑bit cycle counter.
RULE 20: The high half of the 64‑bit counter associated with mcycle is accessed via the separate mcycleh CSR, which returns bits 63‑32 on reads and modifies only those bits on writes when the Sscofpmf extension is implemented.
RULE 21: mcycle is a machine‑mode CSR that counts processor cycles.
RULE 22: mcycle increments by one on every processor cycle regardless of any mcounteren settings.
RULE 23: Reading or writing the mcounteren CSR does not affect the value of mcycle.
RULE 24: The CSR named cycle is a read‑only shadow of mcycle, and any read of cycle returns the current value of mcycle (or the XLEN‑sized portion).
RULE 25: On implementations where XLEN = 32, the CSR cycleh is a read‑only shadow of mcycleh, which holds the upper 32 bits of the full mcycle counter.
RULE 26: The CY bit in mcounteren governs access to the cycle shadow CSR but does not change the incrementing behavior of mcycle, which continues to count in machine mode even when the shadow CSR is inaccessible.
RULE 27: The mcycle CSR increments on every processor cycle unless its counting is inhibited.
RULE 28: The mcycle CSR increments on a cycle when the CY bit of the mcountinhibit register is clear (0).
RULE 29: The mcycle CSR does not increment on a cycle when the CY bit of the mcountinhibit register is set (1).
RULE 30: When multiple harts share a single mcycle CSR, the mcountinhibit.CY field is also shared among those harts.
RULE 31: A write to the mcountinhibit.CY field by any hart that shares the mcycle CSR is observed by all harts sharing that mcycle CSR.
RULE 32: If an implementation does not provide the mcountinhibit register, the implementation behaves as if mcountinhibit.CY were permanently cleared, causing the mcycle CSR to increment on every cycle.
RULE 33: The mcycle CSR increments by exactly one for each CPU cycle.
RULE 34: The CtrCycleCounter increments at the same rate as the mcycle register.
RULE 35: In M-mode, accessing mcycle register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xb00; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 36: In S-mode, accessing mcycle register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xb00; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 37: In U-mode, accessing mcycle register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xb00; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 38: In M-mode, accessing mcycle register while Sscofpmf extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Sscofpmf; csrr a0, 0xb00; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 39: In M-mode, accessing mcycle register while Zicntr extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicntr; csrr a0, 0xb00; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 40: In M-mode, accessing mcycle register while Zicsr extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicsr; csrr a0, 0xb00; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: medeleg
RULE 1: medeleg is a 64‑bit read/write register.
RULE 2: In any hart that implements S‑mode, medeleg must exist.
RULE 3: In any hart that does not implement S‑mode, medeleg should not exist.
RULE 4: Setting a bit in medeleg delegates the synchronous exception whose cause value equals that bit index from U‑mode or S‑mode to the S‑mode trap handler.
RULE 5: medeleg contains a bit for every synchronous exception listed in Table 14, with the bit index equal to the value returned in the mcause register for that exception.
RULE 6: Bits of medeleg that correspond to exceptions that cannot occur in a less‑privileged mode are read‑only zero.
RULE 7: Bit 11 of medeleg is read‑only zero.
RULE 8: Bit 16 of medeleg is read‑only zero because double‑trap is not delegatable.
RULE 9: An implementation shall not make any bit of medeleg read‑only one.
RULE 10: Every delegatable synchronous exception must have its corresponding medeleg bit writable to zero.
RULE 11: An implementation may support only a subset of delegatable exceptions, meaning only a subset of medeleg bits can be set to one.
RULE 12: The set of supported medeleg bits can be discovered by writing 1 to every bit position and then reading back the register; bits that read back as 1 are supported for delegation.
RULE 13: When XLEN = 32, a 32‑bit alias register named medelegh exists, is read/write, and aliases bits 63:32 of medeleg.
RULE 14: When XLEN = 64, the medelegh register does not exist.
RULE 15: In versions 1.9.1 and earlier, medeleg existed but was hard‑wired to zero in M‑mode only (or M/U without N harts).
RULE 16: In those earlier versions, the existence of medeleg is indicated by the misa register, and there is no requirement that medeleg return zero in other privilege modes.
RULE 17: Machine mode uses the medeleg CSR to delegate handling of synchronous exceptions to supervisor mode.
RULE 18: Each bit of medeleg corresponds to a specific synchronous exception code in the same way as the bits of the hypervisor delegation registers hedeleg and hideleg.
RULE 19: The layout of medeleg is identical to the layouts of hedeleg and hideleg.
RULE 20: For each exception, the corresponding bit in medeleg shall be either writable or permanently zero, as defined by the architectural specification for that exception.
RULE 21: The writable‑or‑read‑only‑zero assignments for medeleg bits are the same as those specified for hedeleg.
RULE 22: By default, all traps at any privilege level are handled in machine mode unless the corresponding medeleg bit is set.
RULE 23: When a synchronous trap occurs and the corresponding medeleg bit is set, the trap is taken in supervisor mode instead of machine mode.
RULE 24: Delegation of a trap via medeleg to supervisor mode can be further overridden by additional delegation to virtual‑supervisor mode as controlled by hedeleg.
RULE 25: The medeleg CSR controls the delegation of guest‑page‑fault traps from M‑mode to HS‑mode.
RULE 26: The medeleg CSR must prevent delegation of guest‑page‑fault traps to any privilege mode other than HS‑mode.
RULE 27: The medeleg CSR defines which synchronous traps are delegated from M-mode to HS-mode.
RULE 28: When a trap occurs while the processor is in HS-mode or U-mode and the trap is delegated by medeleg (or by mideleg for interrupts), the trap is taken to HS-mode instead of M-mode.
RULE 29: When a trap occurs while the processor is in HS-mode or U-mode and the trap is not delegated by medeleg (or by mideleg for interrupts), the trap is taken to M-mode.
RULE 30: When a trap occurs while the processor is in VS-mode or VU-mode and the trap is delegated by medeleg (or by mideleg for interrupts), the trap is taken to HS-mode instead of M-mode.
RULE 31: When a trap occurs while the processor is in VS-mode or VU-mode and the trap is not delegated by medeleg (or by mideleg for interrupts), the trap is taken to M-mode.
RULE 32: If a trap that is delegated by medeleg from VS-mode or VU-mode to HS-mode is further delegated by hedeleg or hideleg, the trap is ultimately taken to VS-mode.
RULE 33: In M-mode, accessing medeleg register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x302; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 34: In S-mode, accessing medeleg register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x302; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 35: In U-mode, accessing medeleg register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x302; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 36: In M-mode, setting medeleg.SGPF (bit 23) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800000; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800000].
RULE 37: In M-mode, setting medeleg.VI (bit 22) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400000; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400000].
RULE 38: In M-mode, setting medeleg.LGPF (bit 21) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200000; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200000].
RULE 39: In M-mode, setting medeleg.IGPF (bit 20) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000].
RULE 40: In M-mode, setting medeleg.SPF (bit 15) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000].
RULE 41: In M-mode, setting medeleg.LPF (bit 13) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000].
RULE 42: In M-mode, setting medeleg.IPF (bit 12) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000].
RULE 43: In M-mode, setting medeleg.EM (bit 11) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800].
RULE 44: In M-mode, setting medeleg.EVS (bit 10) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400].
RULE 45: In M-mode, setting medeleg.ES (bit 9) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200].
RULE 46: In M-mode, setting medeleg.EU (bit 8) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100].
RULE 47: In M-mode, setting medeleg.SAF (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 48: In M-mode, setting medeleg.SAM (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 49: In M-mode, setting medeleg.LAF (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 50: In M-mode, setting medeleg.LAM (bit 4) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10].
RULE 51: In M-mode, setting medeleg.B (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 52: In M-mode, setting medeleg.II (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 53: In M-mode, setting medeleg.IAF (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 54: In M-mode, setting medeleg.IAM (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 55: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the medeleg register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x302, a0; csrr a1, 0x302; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 56: In M-mode, accessing medeleg register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x302; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: menvcfg
RULE 1: menvcfg is a machine‑mode xenvcfg CSR that follows the generic format defined for all xenvcfg registers.
RULE 2: menvcfg is a WARL CSR and implementations must define its set of legal encodings via the discovery mechanism, with writes of illegal encodings potentially masked to a legal value.
RULE 3: The CBIE field of menvcfg occupies bits [5:4] and is a WARL field whose encoding 00 causes a CBO.INVAL executed in a non‑machine privilege mode to raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 4: The CBIE field of menvcfg encoding 01 enables a CBO.INVAL executed in a non‑machine privilege mode to perform a cache‑block flush operation.
RULE 5: The CBIE field of menvcfg encoding 10 is reserved and therefore not a legal value.
RULE 6: The CBCFE field of menvcfg occupies bit 6 and is a WARL field where a value of 0 causes a CBO.CLEAN or CBO.FLUSH executed in a non‑machine privilege mode to raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 7: The CBCFE field of menvcfg value 1 enables a CBO.CLEAN or CBO.FLUSH executed in a non‑machine privilege mode to execute normally.
RULE 8: The CBZE field of menvcfg occupies bit 7 and is a WARL field where a value of 0 causes a CBO.ZERO executed in a non‑machine privilege mode to raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 9: The CBZE field of menvcfg value 1 enables a CBO.ZERO executed in a non‑machine privilege mode to execute normally.
RULE 10: When the current privilege mode is not machine mode and menvcfg.CBIE equals 00, an illegal‑instruction exception is raised on execution of a CBO.INVAL instruction.
RULE 11: When menvcfg.CBIE equals 01, a CBO.INVAL instruction executed in a non‑machine privilege mode performs a cache‑block flush operation.
RULE 12: When menvcfg.CBIE holds any legal value other than 01, a CBO.INVAL instruction executed in a non‑machine privilege mode performs a cache‑block invalidate operation.
RULE 13: When the current privilege mode is not machine mode and menvcfg.CBCFE is clear, an illegal‑instruction exception is raised on execution of a CBO.CLEAN or CBO.FLUSH instruction.
RULE 14: When menvcfg.CBCFE is set, a CBO.CLEAN or CBO.FLUSH instruction executed in a non‑machine privilege mode executes normally.
RULE 15: When the current privilege mode is not machine mode and menvcfg.CBZE is clear, an illegal‑instruction exception is raised on execution of a CBO.ZERO instruction.
RULE 16: When menvcfg.CBZE is set, a CBO.ZERO instruction executed in a non‑machine privilege mode executes normally.
RULE 17: The menvcfg register includes an SSE field called menvcfg.SSE.
RULE 18: When the current privilege mode is not Machine mode, execution of the SSAMOSWAP.W instruction must raise an illegal‑instruction exception if menvcfg.SSE equals 0.
RULE 19: When the current privilege mode is not Machine mode, execution of the SSAMOSWAP.D instruction must raise an illegal‑instruction exception if menvcfg.SSE equals 0.
RULE 20: The illegal‑instruction exception check based on menvcfg.SSE is performed before any other privilege‑mode‑specific checks such as the presence of S‑mode or the values of senvcfg.SSE and hen vcfg.SSE.
RULE 21: menvcfg.SSE must be set to 1 in order for SSAMOSWAP.W and SSAMOSWAP.D instructions to be permitted in any non‑Machine privilege mode.
RULE 22: menvcfg is a 64‑bit read/write CSR; when XLEN=32 the 32‑bit read/write alias menvcfgh provides access to menvcfg bits 63:32, and menvcfgh does not exist when XLEN=64.
RULE 23: If U‑mode is not supported, the menvcfg register does not exist.
RULE 24: Bit 0 of menvcfg, named FIOM, is writable.
RULE 25: When menvcfg.FIOM=1, FENCE instructions executed in privilege modes less privileged than M order device I/O accesses together with main memory accesses.
RULE 26: When menvcfg.FIOM=1, the FENCE predecessor bit PI denotes predecessor device input and memory reads (PR implied).
RULE 27: When menvcfg.FIOM=1, the FENCE predecessor bit PO denotes predecessor device output and memory writes (PW implied).
RULE 28: When menvcfg.FIOM=1, the FENCE successor bit SI denotes successor device input and memory reads (SR implied).
RULE 29: When menvcfg.FIOM=1, the FENCE successor bit SO denotes successor device output and memory writes (SW implied).
RULE 30: For privilege modes less privileged than M, if menvcfg.FIOM=1 an atomic instruction that accesses a region ordered as device I/O and has its aq and/or rl bit set is ordered as though it accesses both device I/O and memory.
RULE 31: If S‑mode is not supported or satp.MODE is a read‑only zero, an implementation may make menvcfg.FIOM read‑only zero.
RULE 32: menvcfg must provide a FIOM bit so that M‑mode can emulate the hypervisor extension’s equivalent FIOM bit in henvcfg.
RULE 33: Bit 1 of menvcfg, named PBMTE, controls the Svpbmt extension.
RULE 34: When menvcfg.PBMTE=1, Svpbmt is available for S‑mode and G‑stage address translation.
RULE 35: When menvcfg.PBMTE=0, the implementation behaves as though Svpbmt is not implemented.
RULE 36: If the Svpbmt extension is not implemented, menvcfg.PBMTE is read‑only zero.
RULE 37: With the hypervisor extension present, henvcfg.PBMTE is read‑only zero whenever menvcfg.PBMTE=0.
RULE 38: After software writes a new value to menvcfg.PBMTE, executing SFENCE.VMA with rs1=x0 and rs2=x0 is sufficient to synchronize address‑translation caches for the new interpretation of page‑table entry PBMT fields.
RULE 39: Bit 2 of menvcfg, named ADUE, controls hardware updating of PTE A/D bits when the Svadu extension is implemented.
RULE 40: When menvcfg.ADUE=1, hardware updating of A/D bits is enabled and the implementation behaves as though Svade is not implemented for the corresponding translation stage.
RULE 41: When menvcfg.ADUE=0, the implementation behaves as though Svade is implemented.
RULE 42: If the Svadu extension is not implemented, menvcfg.ADUE is read‑only zero.
RULE 43: With the hypervisor extension present, henvcfg.ADUE is read‑only zero whenever menvcfg.ADUE=0.
RULE 44: Bit 3 of menvcfg, named CDE, controls the Smcdeleg extension.
RULE 45: When menvcfg.CDE=1, the Smcdeleg extension is enabled, allowing Zicntr and Zihpm counters to be delegated to S‑mode.
RULE 46: When menvcfg.CDE=0, the Smcdeleg and Ssccfg extensions appear not to be implemented.
RULE 47: If the Smcdeleg extension is not implemented, menvcfg.CDE is read‑only zero.
RULE 48: Bits 63‑60 of menvcfg form the STCE field, whose semantics are defined by the Sstc extension.
RULE 49: Bit 7 of menvcfg, named CBZE, is defined by the Zicboz extension.
RULE 50: Bit 6 of menvcfg, named CBCFE, is defined by the Zicbom extension.
RULE 51: Bit 5 of menvcfg, named CBIE, is defined by the Zicbom extension.
