RULE 52: Bits 47‑32 of menvcfg form the PMM field, which is defined by the Smnpm extension; each bit of the PMM field is writable but reserved for use by Smnpm.
RULE 53: Bit 4 of menvcfg, named LPE, enables the Zicfilp extension.
RULE 54: When menvcfg.LPE=1 and S‑mode is implemented, Zicfilp is enabled in S‑mode.
RULE 55: When menvcfg.LPE=1 and S‑mode is not implemented, Zicfilp is enabled in U‑mode.
RULE 56: When menvcfg.LPE=0, Zicfilp is not enabled (the same rule applies to S‑mode or, if absent, to U‑mode).
RULE 57: Bit 8 of menvcfg, named SSE, enables the Zicfiss extension.
RULE 58: When menvcfg.SSE=1, Zicfiss is activated in S‑mode.
RULE 59: When menvcfg.SSE=0, 32‑bit Zicfiss instructions behave as defined by Zimop.
RULE 60: When menvcfg.SSE=0, 16‑bit Zicfiss instructions behave as defined by Zcmop.
RULE 61: When menvcfg.SSE=0, the pte.xwr=010b encoding in VS/G‑stage page tables becomes reserved.
RULE 62: When menvcfg.SSE=0, the SSAMOSWAP.W/D instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 63: When menvcfg.SSE=0, the corresponding henvcfg.SSE and senvcfg.SSE fields are read‑only zero.
RULE 64: Bit 61 of menvcfg, named DTE, is defined by the Ssdbltrp extension.
RULE 65: When menvcfg.DTE=0, the implementation behaves as though the Ssdbltrp extension is not implemented.
RULE 66: If the Ssdbltrp extension is not implemented, sstatus.SDT, vsstatus.SDT, and henvcfg.DTE bits are read‑only zero.
RULE 67: All bits of menvcfg that are not explicitly assigned to a defined field are WPRI, meaning writes to those bits are ignored and reads return zero.
RULE 68: The machine‑level CSR menvcfg contains fields that can be used to disable individual architectural extensions for S‑mode.
RULE 69: When software writes menvcfg to set a field that disables an extension for S‑mode, the implementation must treat that extension as not implemented at the supervisor privilege level.
RULE 70: If an extension has been disabled for S‑mode via menvcfg, any supervisor‑mode access to the extension’s resources, such as the indirect‑access CSRs siselect or sireg*, must be processed as if the extension were absent for S‑mode.
RULE 71: When the Smcdeleg or Ssccfg extension is implemented, menvcfg.CDE must be set to 1 in order to enable M‑mode to delegate selected hardware performance counters to S‑mode.
RULE 72: While executing in M‑mode or S‑mode, any attempt to access a sireg* CSR causes an illegal‑instruction exception if menvcfg.CDE = 0.
RULE 73: While executing in VS‑mode, an attempt to access any sireg* CSR (mapped to vsireg*) causes an illegal‑instruction exception if menvcfg.CDE = 0.
RULE 74: While executing in VS‑mode, an attempt to access any sireg* CSR (mapped to vsireg*) causes a virtual‑instruction exception if menvcfg.CDE = 1.
RULE 75: The menvcfg CSR contains a field named CDE.
RULE 76: If the CDE field in the menvcfg register is zero, every attempt to access the scountinhibit register must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 77: The menvcfg register contains a field named CDE.
RULE 78: When menvcfg.CDE is set to 1, any attempt to read the scountovf CSR from VS-mode or VU-mode raises a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 79: The exception behavior caused by menvcfg.CDE = 1 is defined only for implementations that support Smcdeleg/Ssccfg, Sscofpmf, and the H extension.
RULE 80: If the SSE field of the menvcfg CSR is 1, executing the SSAMOSWAP.W/D instruction in U-mode raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 81: If the SSE field of the menvcfg CSR is 1, executing the SSAMOSWAP.W/D instruction in VU-mode raises a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 82: The menvcfg CSR includes an ADUE field.
RULE 83: When the Svadu extension is implemented, the ADUE field in menvcfg is writable.
RULE 84: menvcfg bit 63, named STCE, determines whether the stimecmp register is enabled for S‑mode.
RULE 85: If the extension that defines STCE is not implemented, menvcfg STCE is hard‑wired to zero and cannot be set to one.
RULE 86: menvcfg STCE is a WARL field, so writes to it are masked to a value that the implementation supports.
RULE 87: When the extension is implemented and menvcfg STCE is zero, any access to stimecmp in S‑mode or vstimecmp in VS‑mode from a privilege level other than M‑mode raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 88: When the extension is implemented and menvcfg STCE is zero, the STCE bit in henvcfg is read‑only zero.
RULE 89: When the extension is implemented and menvcfg STCE is zero, the STIP bits in mip and sip behave as defined by the base specification, ignoring the stimecmp extension.
RULE 90: When the H extension is present and menvcfg STCE is zero, the hip.VSTIP bit behaves as defined by the base specification, ignoring the stimecmp extension.
RULE 91: When menvcfg STCE is one and henvcfg STCE is zero, an access to the stimecmp register (aliased to vstimecmp) while the virtual‑memory mode bit V equals one raises a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 92: When menvcfg STCE is one and henvcfg STCE is zero, the hip.VSTIP bit reverts to its base‑specification behavior, ignoring the stimecmp extension.
RULE 93: When the SSE bit in the menvcfg register is set to 1, executing the SSAMOSWAP.W or SSAMOSWAP.D instruction raises a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 94: The menvcfg CSR contains a PBMTE bit.
RULE 95: When the PBMTE bit in menvcfg is changed, software must execute an HFENCE.GVMA instruction with rs1 = x0 and rs2 = x0 to perform the required synchronization.
RULE 96: Executing HFENCE.GVMA with rs1 = x0 and rs2 = x0 synchronizes the system with respect to the altered interpretation of PBMT fields in both G‑stage and VS‑stage page‑table entries.
RULE 97: The menvcfg register contains a readable LPE bit that reports the landing‑pad‑enabled state for the privilege modes that consult it.
RULE 98: If S‑mode is implemented, the architectural xLPE state for S‑mode is equal to the value of menvcfg.LPE.
RULE 99: If S‑mode is implemented, the architectural xLPE state for HS‑mode is equal to the value of menvcfg.LPE.
RULE 100: If S‑mode is not implemented, the architectural xLPE state for U‑mode is equal to the value of menvcfg.LPE.
RULE 101: The value of menvcfg.LPE is used directly, without alteration, to compute the xLPE state for each privilege mode that references it.
RULE 102: The menvcfg.LPE bit by itself does not enable the Zicfilp extension; the Zicfilp extension must be explicitly enabled for each privilege mode.
RULE 103: When menvcfg.LPE contributes to a true xLPE state for a privilege mode, forward‑edge control‑flow integrity using landing pads is enabled for that mode.
RULE 104: The menvcfg CSR includes an SSE field.
RULE 105: When the current privilege mode is lower than machine mode and the menvcfg.SSE field is 0, any attempt to access the ssp CSR must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 106: The menvcfg register contains a field named menvcfg.SSE that controls the Shadow‑Stack‑Enabled setting.
RULE 107: When S‑mode is implemented, the value of menvcfg.SSE directly determines the xSSE state for the S privilege mode.
RULE 108: When S‑mode is implemented, the value of menvcfg.SSE directly determines the xSSE state for the HS privilege mode.
RULE 109: The xSSE state, as derived from menvcfg.SSE, indicates whether the Shadow‑Stack‑Enabled functionality provided by the Zicfiss extension is enabled for that privilege mode.
RULE 110: The specification does not define any reset value, writeability constraints, or side effects for the menvcfg register.
RULE 111: The menvcfg CSR contains a field named SSE that controls the shadow‑stack enable setting.
RULE 112: When the SSE field of menvcfg is 0, the page‑table permission encoding R=0, W=1, X=0 remains reserved and must not be used to designate a page as a shadow‑stack page.
RULE 113: When the Ssdbltrp extension is implemented, the menvcfg register contains a field named .DTE.
RULE 114: The .DTE field of the menvcfg register is introduced by the Ssdbltrp double‑trap extension.
RULE 115: Bits 33:32 of the menvcfg CSR constitute a 2‑bit WARL field named PMM.
RULE 116: Writing a value to the PMM field of menvcfg enables or disables pointer masking for the next lower privilege mode.
RULE 117: The next lower privilege mode referenced by menvcfg.PMM is S‑mode (or HS‑mode) if S‑mode is implemented, otherwise U‑mode.
RULE 118: Each possible encoding of the PMM field in menvcfg specifies a normative pointer‑masking action defined by the architecture.
RULE 119: The type of address being processed determines which kind of pointer masking is applied as dictated by menvcfg.
RULE 120: When executing in VS or VU virtualized mode with vsatp.MODE set to Bare, menvcfg causes the architecture to apply physical‑address pointer masking by zero‑extension.
RULE 121: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 122: In S-mode, accessing menvcfg register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 123: In U-mode, accessing menvcfg register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 124: In M-mode, setting menvcfg.STCE (bit 63) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000000000; csrs 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000].
RULE 125: In M-mode, setting menvcfg.PBMTE (bit 62) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000000000000000; csrs 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000000000000000].
RULE 126: In M-mode, setting menvcfg.ADUE (bit 61) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000000000000000; csrs 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000000000000000].
RULE 127: In M-mode, setting menvcfg.CBZE (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 128: In M-mode, setting menvcfg.CBCFE (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 129: In M-mode, setting menvcfg.CBIE (bit 4) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10; csrs 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10].
RULE 130: In M-mode, setting menvcfg.FIOM (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 131: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the menvcfg register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x30a, a0; csrr a1, 0x30a; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 132: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Smcdeleg extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smcdeleg; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 133: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Smnpm extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smnpm; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 134: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Ssccfg extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Ssccfg; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 135: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Ssdbltrp extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Ssdbltrp; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 136: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Sstc extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Sstc; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 137: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Svadu extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Svadu; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 138: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Svpbmt extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Svpbmt; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 139: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Zicbom extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicbom; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 140: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Zicboz extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicboz; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 141: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Zicfilp extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicfilp; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 142: In M-mode, accessing menvcfg register while Zicfiss extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicfiss; csrr a0, 0x30a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mepc
RULE 1: When a double‑trap occurs and the Smrnmi extension is implemented with mnstatus.NMIE equal to 1, the hardware writes the value that the unexpected trap would have written to the mepc register into the mnepc CSR.
RULE 2: When a trap is delegated to S-mode, the mepc register is not written and therefore retains its previous contents.
RULE 3: The mepc register is an MXLEN‑bit read/write CSR.
RULE 4: The low bit mepc[0] is always zero regardless of implementation.
RULE 5: On implementations that support only IALIGN=32, both low bits mepc[1:0] are always zero.
RULE 6: If IALIGN=32, reads of the mepc register mask bit mepc[1] so that it appears as zero.
RULE 7: The read of mepc performed implicitly by the MRET instruction also masks bit mepc[1] when IALIGN=32.
RULE 8: Even when IALIGN=32 masks mepc[1] on reads, the bit mepc[1] remains writable.
RULE 9: The mepc register is a Write‑Any‑Legal‑Value (WARL) register that must be able to hold all valid virtual addresses.
RULE 10: The mepc register is not required to hold all possible invalid virtual addresses.
RULE 11: Before a write to mepc, an implementation may convert an invalid address into another invalid address that the register can represent.
RULE 12: When address translation is disabled, the mepc register must be capable of representing any physical address that could serve as a valid program counter or effective address.
RULE 13: On taking a trap into M‑mode, the hardware writes mepc with the virtual address of the interrupted or faulting instruction.
RULE 14: In any situation other than a trap taken into M‑mode, the hardware does not automatically write mepc; software may write it explicitly.
RULE 15: When an instruction access‑fault or page‑fault exception occurs on a hart that supports variable‑length instructions, mepc is set to the address of the first byte of the faulting instruction.
RULE 16: For an illegal‑instruction exception, mepc is set to the address of the offending instruction in memory.
RULE 17: Software can load from instruction memory at the address contained in mepc to determine whether a zero mtval indicates that faulting instruction bits are unavailable or that an illegal zero instruction was fetched.
RULE 18: When an enabled interrupt becomes pending while the hart is stalled by a WFI instruction, the trap is taken on the following instruction and the architectural register mepc is written with the value pc + 4.
RULE 19: Executing the mret instruction restores the program counter from mepc, so a trap entry that set mepc to pc + 4 causes execution to resume at the instruction immediately after the WFI.
RULE 20: When a non‑maskable interrupt occurs, the mepc register is written with the virtual address of the instruction that was interrupted.
RULE 21: If a non‑maskable interrupt occurs while a machine‑mode interrupt handler is active, the mepc register may be overwritten, discarding the value previously saved by the earlier handler.
RULE 22: On a non‑maskable interrupt, the processor jumps to a handler in machine mode and overwrites the current mepc register value.
RULE 23: Because the mepc register is overwritten on a non‑maskable interrupt, any previous mepc value is not recoverable when the hart was executing machine‑mode code in a trap handler at the time of the interrupt.
RULE 24: Under the base architecture, execution is not generally resumable after a non‑maskable interrupt because the original mepc cannot be restored.
RULE 25: When a trap is taken into M‑mode, the hardware writes the CSR mepc.
RULE 26: The hardware write to the CSR mepc occurs as part of the trap‑entry actions defined for M‑mode.
RULE 27: When the MRET instruction is executed, it sets the program counter (pc) to the value contained in the mepc register.
RULE 28: In M-mode, accessing mepc register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x341; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 29: In S-mode, accessing mepc register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x341; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 30: In U-mode, accessing mepc register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x341; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 31: In M-mode, setting mepc.PC (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x341, a0; csrr a1, 0x341; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 32: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mepc register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x341, a0; csrr a1, 0x341; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 33: In M-mode, accessing mepc register while Smrnmi extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smrnmi; csrr a0, 0x341; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mhartid
RULE 1: mhartid is a read‑only CSR whose width equals MXLEN bits and whose value encodes the integer identifier of the hart currently executing code.
RULE 2: mhartid must be readable in every RISC‑V implementation.
RULE 3: Any attempt to write to mhartid must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 4: The hart identifier returned by mhartid for each hart must be unique across all harts in the execution environment.
RULE 5: The set of identifiers returned by mhartid across harts may contain gaps; contiguity is not required.
RULE 6: At least one hart must return the identifier zero when mhartid is read.
RULE 7: In M-mode, accessing mhartid register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xf14; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 8: In S-mode, accessing mhartid register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xf14; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 9: In U-mode, accessing mhartid register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xf14; # Result: mcause = 0x2].

REGISTER: mhpmcounter31
RULE 1: mhpmcounter31 counts occurrences of the event selected by the 64‑bit WARL CSR mhpmevent31.
RULE 2: mhpmcounter31 is implemented as a 64‑bit counter on both RV32 and RV64 implementations.
RULE 3: mhpmcounter31 is a WARL (Write‑Any‑Read‑Legal) register that supports up to 64 bits of precision.
RULE 4: After a hart reset, mhpmcounter31 holds an arbitrary implementation‑defined value, and software may write any value to it.
RULE 5: A write to mhpmcounter31 takes effect after the writing instruction has otherwise completed.
RULE 6: On RV32 implementations, a normal CSR write to mhpmcounter31 updates only bits 31‑0, while bits 63‑32 are written via the paired CSR mhpmcounter31h when present.
RULE 7: When XLEN=64, a read of mhpmcounter31 returns the full 64‑bit counter value.
RULE 8: When XLEN=32, a read of mhpmcounter31 returns bits 31‑0, and the high word bits 63‑32 are read from the paired CSR mhpmcounter31h.
RULE 9: A legal implementation may make mhpmcounter31 read‑only and hard‑wired to zero, causing reads to return zero and writes to be ignored, and its selector mhpmevent31 also returns zero.
RULE 10: Event 0 defined in mhpmevent31 represents "no event," which prevents mhpmcounter31 from incrementing.
RULE 11: Implementations must provide mhpmcounter31, but may optionally implement it as a read‑only zero counter.
RULE 12: mhpmcounter31 has no defined sharing semantics across harts; each hart’s mhpmcounter31 operates independently.
RULE 13: mhpmcounter31 increments each time the event selected by mhpmevent31 occurs, as defined by the platform.
RULE 14: In M-mode, accessing mhpmcounter31 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xb1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 15: In S-mode, accessing mhpmcounter31 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xb1f; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 16: In U-mode, accessing mhpmcounter31 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xb1f; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 17: In M-mode, accessing mhpmcounter31 register while Zihpm extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zihpm; csrr a0, 0xb1f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mhpmevent31
RULE 1: mhpmevent31 is a 64‑bit WARL CSR that serves as the event‑selector for hardware performance counter 31.
RULE 2: Implementations may elect to implement mhpmevent31 as a read‑only zero register.
RULE 3: If mhpmevent31 is writable, it must accept any 64‑bit value on write and return a legal value on subsequent reads.
RULE 4: Writing a value to mhpmevent31 selects the hardware event that causes the associated counter mhpmcounter31 to increment.
RULE 5: An event code of 0 written to mhpmevent31 denotes "no event".
RULE 6: After a hart reset, mhpmevent31 contains an arbitrary (implementation‑defined) value.
RULE 7: A write to mhpmevent31 takes effect only after the writing instruction has otherwise completed.
RULE 8: When XLEN=32, reads of mhpmevent31 return the lower 32 bits (bits 31‑0) of the underlying 64‑bit value.
RULE 9: When XLEN=32, writes to mhpmevent31 modify only the lower 32 bits (bits 31‑0) of the underlying 64‑bit value.
RULE 10: The high‑half CSR mhpmevent31h, which provides access to the upper 32 bits of mhpmevent31, is implemented only if the Sscofpmf extension is present.
RULE 11: A conforming implementation may choose to make both mhpmcounter31 and mhpmevent31 read‑only zero, thereby disabling the counter and its selector.
RULE 12: The mhpmevent31 CSR is the machine‑mode hardware‑performance‑monitoring event‑selector register with index 31.
RULE 13: Accessing the hpmevent31 subset through the indirect CSR interface (sireg5) reads or writes the same subset of bits that are contained in the mhpmevent31 CSR.
RULE 14: Indirect access to mhpmevent31 via sireg5 is permitted only when the machine‑mode menvcfg.CDE bit is set to 1.
RULE 15: Indirect access to mhpmevent31 via sireg5 is permitted only when the mcounteren bit for counter 31 is set to 1.
RULE 16: Indirect access to mhpmevent31 via sireg5 is permitted only when the privileged‑mode‑access block bit mstateen0[60] (and hstateen0[60] if implemented) is set to 1.
RULE 17: Indirect access to mhpmevent31 via sireg5 is permitted only when the current XLEN is 32; attempts with XLEN = 64 raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 18: Indirect access to mhpmevent31 via sireg5 is permitted only if the implementation includes the architectural extension that defines mhpmevent31; otherwise an illegal‑instruction exception is raised.
RULE 19: When the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, bit 62 (MINH) of the mhpmevent31 subset accessed through sireg5 is read‑only and always yields 0.
RULE 20: An illegal‑instruction exception is raised on any indirect access to mhpmevent31 via sireg5 if any of the required conditions (CDE = 0, mcounteren[31] = 0, XLEN = 64, missing required extension, or mstateen0[60] = 0) is violated.
RULE 21: A virtual‑instruction exception is raised for VS‑mode or VU‑mode attempts to indirectly access mhpmevent31 via sireg5 when the hypervisor extension is present.
RULE 22: A successful indirect read of mhpmevent31 via sireg5 returns only the bits of the underlying mhpmevent31 CSR that are defined as readable in the hpmevent31 subset.
RULE 23: A successful indirect write to mhpmevent31 via sireg5 updates only the bits of the underlying mhpmevent31 CSR that are defined as writable in the hpmevent31 subset, leaving all other bits unchanged.
RULE 24: The CSR mhpmevent31 is one of the mhpmevent registers defined in the architecture, covering the range from mhpmevent3 through mhpmevent31.
RULE 25: The mhpmevent31 register contains an overflow (OF) bit.
RULE 26: The overflow (OF) bit of mhpmevent31 is mirrored into bit 31 of the scountovf CSR.
RULE 27: In M‑mode, the overflow bit of mhpmevent31, as reflected in scountovf bit 31, is always readable.
RULE 28: In S‑mode and HS‑mode, the overflow bit of mhpmevent31, as reflected in scountovf bit 31, is readable only if mcounteren bit 31 is set; otherwise it reads as zero.
RULE 29: In VS‑mode, the overflow bit of mhpmevent31, as reflected in scountovf bit 31, is readable only if both mcounteren bit 31 and hcounteren bit 31 are set; otherwise it reads as zero.
RULE 30: In M-mode, accessing mhpmevent31 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x33f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 31: In S-mode, accessing mhpmevent31 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x33f; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 32: In U-mode, accessing mhpmevent31 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x33f; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 33: In M-mode, setting mhpmevent31.OF (bit 63) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000000000; csrs 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000].
RULE 34: In M-mode, setting mhpmevent31.MINH (bit 62) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000000000000000; csrs 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000000000000000].
RULE 35: In M-mode, setting mhpmevent31.SINH (bit 61) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000000000000000; csrs 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000000000000000].
RULE 36: In M-mode, setting mhpmevent31.UINH (bit 60) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000000000000; csrs 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000000000000].
RULE 37: In M-mode, setting mhpmevent31.VSINH (bit 59) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800000000000000; csrs 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800000000000000].
RULE 38: In M-mode, setting mhpmevent31.VUINH (bit 58) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400000000000000; csrs 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400000000000000].
RULE 39: In M-mode, setting mhpmevent31.EVENT (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 40: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mhpmevent31 register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x33f, a0; csrr a1, 0x33f; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 41: In M-mode, accessing mhpmevent31 register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x33f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 42: In M-mode, accessing mhpmevent31 register while S extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: S; csrr a0, 0x33f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 43: In M-mode, accessing mhpmevent31 register while Sscofpmf extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Sscofpmf; csrr a0, 0x33f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 44: In M-mode, accessing mhpmevent31 register while Zihpm extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zihpm; csrr a0, 0x33f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mideleg
RULE 1: mideleg is an MXLEN‑bit read/write register.
RULE 2: mideleg must be present on every hart that implements Supervisor mode.
RULE 3: mideleg should not be present on any hart that does not implement Supervisor mode.
RULE 4: Setting bit i of mideleg delegates the interrupt whose position i matches the same position in the mip register to Supervisor mode.
RULE 5: When an interrupt delegated via mideleg is taken, that interrupt is masked at Machine privilege level and cannot be taken while executing in Machine mode.
RULE 6: The bit positions of mideleg correspond exactly to the bit positions of the mip register.
RULE 7: Bit 5 of mideleg controls delegation of the Supervisor‑Timer Interrupt (STIP).
RULE 8: An implementation may support only a subset of delegatable interrupts in mideleg.
RULE 9: Software can discover which bits of mideleg are writable by writing 1 to every bit position and reading back mideleg; bits that read back as 1 indicate support for delegation of the corresponding interrupt.
RULE 10: An implementation shall not fix any bit of mideleg that corresponds to a machine‑level interrupt as read‑only 1.
RULE 11: Bits of mideleg that correspond to lower‑privilege interrupts may be implementation‑defined read‑only 1.
RULE 12: In architectural versions 1.11 and earlier, all bits of mideleg are required to be read/write with no read‑only‑1 bits.
RULE 13: When an interrupt is delegated through mideleg to Supervisor mode, the Supervisor‑mode trap registers (scause, sepc, stval, etc.) are written according to delegated‑trap handling while the Machine‑mode trap registers (mcause, mepc, mtval, MPP, MPIE) are not written.
RULE 14: The existence of mideleg is indicated by the misa register; if misa indicates that mideleg does not exist, any architectural read or write of mideleg is undefined.
RULE 15: An interrupt i is prevented from trapping to M‑mode when the implemented mideleg register has bit i set, because that setting delegates the interrupt to a lower privilege mode.
RULE 16: When the implemented mideleg register has bit i set, the corresponding interrupt i becomes visible as bit i in the sip register.
RULE 17: When the implemented mideleg register has bit i set, the corresponding interrupt i can be masked using bit i in the sie register.
RULE 18: If the implemented mideleg register has bit i clear, then bits i in both sip and sie are read‑only zero.
RULE 19: If the mideleg register is not implemented, condition (c) of the interrupt trapping rule is considered satisfied for all interrupts, meaning no delegation occurs via mideleg.
RULE 20: The mideleg register must not influence whether a hart executing the Wait‑for‑Interrupt (WFI) instruction resumes execution.
RULE 21: If a locally enabled interrupt becomes pending, a hart executing WFI must resume execution even when that interrupt is delegated to a less‑privileged mode by the mideleg register.
RULE 22: The mideleg CSR may contain a Local‑Counter‑Overflow Interrupt (LCOFI) bit positioned at bit 13.
RULE 23: An implementation may omit the LCOFI bit from the mideleg CSR.
RULE 24: If the LCOFI bit is implemented in the mideleg CSR, that bit is writable by software.
RULE 25: When the implemented LCOFI bit in the mideleg CSR is set, all LCOFIs are delegated to S‑mode.
RULE 26: The mideleg register determines whether the local count‑overflow interrupt (LCOFI) is delegated to S‑mode.
RULE 27: If the mideleg register does not delegate the local count‑overflow interrupt (LCOFI), the interrupt is handled in M‑mode.
RULE 28: M‑mode uses the mideleg CSR to delegate a subset of traps (interrupts) from machine mode to hypervisor‑supervisor (HS) mode.
RULE 29: An interrupt that has been delegated to HS‑mode using mideleg may be further delegated to VS‑mode only if the corresponding bit in the hideleg register is set.
RULE 30: The bit‑field layout of the mideleg register is identical to the layouts of the hedeleg and hideleg registers.
RULE 31: The hypervisor extension augments or modifies the machine CSR mideleg.
RULE 32: When the hypervisor extension is implemented, bits 10, 6, and 2 of the mideleg register are hard‑wired to 1 and cannot be cleared by software.
RULE 33: If the GEILEN field is non‑zero, indicating that guest external interrupts are implemented, bit 12 of the mideleg register is hard‑wired to 1 and cannot be cleared by software.
RULE 34: All VS‑level interrupt bits in the mideleg register cause those interrupts to be delegated past M‑mode to HS‑mode.
RULE 35: The guest external interrupt bit in the mideleg register (bit 12) causes that interrupt to be delegated past M‑mode to HS‑mode.
RULE 36: For any bit position where the mideleg register contains a 0, the corresponding bits in the hideleg, hip, and hie registers are hard‑wired to 0 and cannot be set by software.
RULE 37: When a trap occurs while the processor is in HS‑mode, U‑mode, VS‑mode, or VU‑mode, the hardware consults the mideleg CSR to determine whether the trap cause is delegated.
RULE 38: If the trap cause is set in mideleg (or in medeleg), the trap is taken to HS‑mode instead of the default destination M‑mode.
RULE 39: For traps originating in VS‑mode or VU‑mode, a cause delegated by mideleg (or medeleg) routes the trap to HS‑mode unless a further delegation for the same cause exists in hedeleg or hideleg.
RULE 40: If a trap originating in VS‑mode or VU‑mode has its cause delegated by both mideleg (or medeleg) and also by hedeleg or hideleg, the trap is taken to VS‑mode rather than HS‑mode.
RULE 41: In M-mode, accessing mideleg register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x303; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 42: In S-mode, accessing mideleg register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x303; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 43: In U-mode, accessing mideleg register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x303; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 44: In M-mode, setting mideleg.SSI (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 45: In M-mode, setting mideleg.VSSI (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 46: In M-mode, setting mideleg.MSI (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 47: In M-mode, setting mideleg.STI (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 48: In M-mode, setting mideleg.VSTI (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 49: In M-mode, setting mideleg.MTI (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 50: In M-mode, setting mideleg.SEI (bit 9) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200].
RULE 51: In M-mode, setting mideleg.VSEI (bit 10) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400].
RULE 52: In M-mode, setting mideleg.MEI (bit 11) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800].
RULE 53: In M-mode, setting mideleg.SGEI (bit 12) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000].
RULE 54: In M-mode, setting mideleg.LCOFI (bit 13) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000; csrs 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000].
RULE 55: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mideleg register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x303, a0; csrr a1, 0x303; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 56: In M-mode, accessing mideleg register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x303; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 57: In M-mode, accessing mideleg register while S extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: S; csrr a0, 0x303; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: minstret
RULE 1: The machine‑mode CSR minstret counts the number of instructions retired by the hart.
RULE 2: The minstret counter provides 64‑bit precision on both RV32 and RV64 implementations.
RULE 3: After a hart reset the initial value of minstret is implementation‑defined and may be arbitrary.
RULE 4: The minstret CSR is writable, and a software write may set it to any desired value.
RULE 5: A write to the minstret CSR takes effect only after the writing instruction has otherwise completed.
RULE 6: On a hart with XLEN = 32, accesses to the CSR named minstret affect only bits 31‑0 of the 64‑bit minstret counter.
RULE 7: On a hart with XLEN = 32, reading the CSR named minstret returns the low 32 bits of the 64‑bit minstret counter.
RULE 8: On a hart with XLEN = 32, writing the CSR named minstret updates only bits 31‑0 of the 64‑bit minstret counter, leaving bits 63‑32 unchanged.
RULE 9: When the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, the high 32 bits of the 64‑bit minstret counter are accessible via the separate CSR minstreth.
RULE 10: The high‑half CSR minstreth does not affect the behavior of the CSR named minstret.
RULE 11: The instret CSR provides a read‑only view of the lower XLEN bits of the minstret counter.
RULE 12: When XLEN equals 32, the upper 32 bits of the minstret counter are exposed as the minstreh register.
RULE 13: The instreth CSR provides a read‑only view of the minstreh register, which reflects the high‑order bits of minstret when XLEN is 32.
RULE 14: If the IR bit in the mcounteren CSR is clear, any attempt to read the instret CSR in S‑mode or U‑mode raises an illegal‑instruction exception, preventing visibility of minstret.
RULE 15: If the IR bit in the mcounteren CSR is set, reads of the instret CSR are permitted in the next lower implemented privilege mode (S‑mode if present, otherwise U‑mode), allowing access to minstret.
RULE 16: The minstret counter increments continuously regardless of the current setting of the mcounteren CSR, and accesses to mcounteren do not modify the counter value.
RULE 17: minstret increments on each retired instruction when the IR bit in mcountinhibit is clear.
RULE 18: minstret does not increment on retired instructions when the IR bit in mcountinhibit is set.
RULE 19: If the mcountinhibit CSR is not implemented, the implementation must behave as if the CSR were zero, causing minstret to increment normally.
RULE 20: Setting the IR bit in mcountinhibit can be used to inhibit minstret in order to reduce energy consumption.
RULE 21: Inhibiting minstret (and mcycle) via mcountinhibit enables an atomic snapshot of their values.
RULE 22: When an ECALL instruction raises a synchronous exception, it is not considered retired and therefore must not increment minstret.
RULE 23: When an EBREAK instruction raises a synchronous exception, it is not considered retired and therefore must not increment minstret.
RULE 24: When siselect = 0x42, a read or write of the low‑part sireg accesses the minstret counter, and a read or write of sireg4 accesses the high part of the minstret counter.
RULE 25: When siselect = 0x42, accesses of sireg2 and sireg5 read or write the instretcfg and instretcfgh CSRs, which constitute a subset of the state defined by the minstretcfg CSR.
RULE 26: Access to any sireg register while siselect = 0x42 is illegal if the minstret bit in mcounteren is 0, meaning the minstret counter has not been delegated to S‑mode.
RULE 27: If menvcfg.CDE = 0, any attempt to access a sireg register while siselect = 0x42 triggers an illegal‑instruction exception for the minstret counter.
RULE 28: When XLEN = 64, any access to sireg4 or sireg5 while siselect = 0x42 causes an illegal‑instruction exception for the high half of the minstret counter.
RULE 29: If the Smstateen extension is implemented and mstateen0[60] = 0, accesses to siselect and all sireg registers that map to the minstret counter from any privilege level less privileged than M‑mode raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 30: With the H extension present, VS‑mode accesses to sireg* that alias vsireg* and map to the minstret counter are subject to the same delegation and CDE checks as in M‑ and S‑mode.
RULE 31: Under the H extension, a VS‑mode attempt to access a sireg* that maps to the minstret counter raises an illegal‑instruction exception if menvcfg.CDE = 0, and raises a virtual‑instruction exception if menvcfg.CDE = 1.
RULE 32: If any architectural extension required for the minstret state (such as the instret counter implementation) is not implemented, an M‑mode or S‑mode access to the minstret state via sireg* raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 33: In M-mode, accessing minstret register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xb02; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 34: In S-mode, accessing minstret register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xb02; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 35: In U-mode, accessing minstret register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xb02; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 36: In M-mode, accessing minstret register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0xb02; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 37: In M-mode, accessing minstret register while Smstateen extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smstateen; csrr a0, 0xb02; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 38: In M-mode, accessing minstret register while Sscofpmf extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Sscofpmf; csrr a0, 0xb02; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mscratch
RULE 1: mscratch is a machine‑mode control and status register (CSR).
RULE 2: mscratch has a width of MXLEN bits, matching the implementation’s general‑purpose register size.
RULE 3: mscratch is readable and writable by software executing in machine mode.
RULE 4: mscratch is accessible only in machine mode and cannot be accessed from lower privilege levels.
RULE 5: mscratch is typically used to hold a pointer to a machine‑mode hart‑local context space.
RULE 6: When an M‑mode trap handler is entered, the current value in mscratch is normally exchanged with a user register using a CSR‑swap instruction.
RULE 7: CSR‑swap instructions allow the operating system to quickly save a value into mscratch and later restore it.
RULE 8: The operating system can rely on mscratch to retain a saved value while user code executes.
RULE 9: In M-mode, accessing mscratch register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x340; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 10: In S-mode, accessing mscratch register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x340; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 11: In U-mode, accessing mscratch register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x340; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 12: In M-mode, accessing mscratch register while Zicsr extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicsr; csrr a0, 0x340; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mstatus
RULE 1: If the TW bit in mstatus is set and a WRS.NTO instruction is executed in any privilege mode other than M mode, and the instruction does not complete within the implementation‑specific bounded time limit, an illegal‑instruction exception is raised.
RULE 2: If a WRS.NTO instruction is executed in VS mode or VU mode, the VTW bit in hstatus is set, the TW bit in mstatus is clear, and the instruction does not complete within the implementation‑specific bounded time limit, a virtual‑instruction exception is raised.
RULE 3: The mstatus CSR contains the MPRV, MPV, MPP, SUM, and MXR bits, all of which affect memory‑access permissions.
RULE 4: The MPRV bit in mstatus controls whether address translation uses the current privilege mode or the privilege mode specified by MPP for memory accesses.
RULE 5: The MPV bit in mstatus contributes to the determination of memory‑access permissions for load, store, and CMO instructions.
RULE 6: The MPP field in mstatus records the machine previous privilege mode and influences the privilege level used for address translation and permission checks.
RULE 7: The SUM bit in mstatus permits supervisor‑mode code to access memory locations that are otherwise restricted to user mode.
RULE 8: The MXR bit in mstatus makes instruction‑fetch‑only (executable) pages also readable for data accesses.
RULE 9: When executing a cache‑block‑management (CMO) instruction, the hardware consults the MPRV, MPV, and MPP bits in mstatus to decide whether the instruction may access the target cache block.
RULE 10: When executing a CMO instruction, the hardware also consults the SUM and MXR bits in mstatus (together with the corresponding bits in sstatus and vsstatus) to decide whether the instruction may access the target cache block.
RULE 11: The permission outcome for a CMO instruction is determined by the values of the MPRV, MPV, MPP, SUM, and MXR bits in mstatus, exactly as for load or store instructions.
RULE 12: When the Zfinx extension is implemented, the FS field of mstatus is hard‑wired to 0.
RULE 13: When the FS field of mstatus is hard‑wired to 0, it does not affect the trapping behavior of floating‑point instructions or accesses to the fcsr register.
RULE 14: The vector context status field VS is defined to occupy bits 10 and 9 of the mstatus register.
RULE 15: The VS field that resides in mstatus bits 10 and 9 is mirrored in bits 10 and 9 of the sstatus register.
RULE 16: The VS field in mstatus can contain only the values Off, Initial, Clean, or Dirty.
RULE 17: When mstatus.VS equals Off, any attempt to execute a vector instruction or to access a vector CSR raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 18: If mstatus.VS is Initial or Clean, execution of any instruction that changes vector state, including accesses to vector CSRs, must set mstatus.VS to Dirty.
RULE 19: An implementation may change mstatus.VS from Initial or Clean to Dirty at any time, even if no vector‑state‑changing instruction has been executed.
RULE 20: When mstatus.VS is Dirty, the mstatus.SD bit must be set to 1.
RULE 21: When mstatus.VS is not Dirty, the value of mstatus.SD is determined by the existing specifications based on other status fields.
RULE 22: The mstatus.VS field may be implemented even when the misa.V bit is zero.
RULE 23: The mstatus.VS field acts as a vector‑context status field that is defined analogously to the floating‑point FS field.
RULE 24: If the V bit is 1 and either mstatus.VS or vsstatus.VS is Off, any attempt to execute a vector instruction or to access a vector CSR raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 25: If the V bit is 1 and both mstatus.VS and vsstatus.VS are not Off, execution of any instruction that changes vector state or accesses a vector CSR must set mstatus.VS to Dirty.
RULE 26: An implementation may change mstatus.VS from Initial or Clean to Dirty at any time, even when no vector‑state change occurs.
RULE 27: When mstatus.VS is Dirty, the mstatus.SD bit must be 1.
RULE 28: When mstatus.VS is not Dirty, the value of mstatus.SD follows the setting prescribed by the existing specifications.
RULE 29: The mstatus register includes a floating‑point unit status field named mstatus.FS.
RULE 30: If mstatus.FS is Off, any attempt to execute a vector floating‑point instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 31: Any vector floating‑point instruction that modifies any floating‑point extension state must set mstatus.FS to Dirty.
RULE 32: When the hypervisor extension is implemented and the virtualization mode bit V equals 1, if mstatus.FS is Off then any attempt to execute a vector floating‑point instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 33: When the hypervisor extension is implemented and V equals 1, any vector floating‑point instruction that modifies floating‑point extension state must set mstatus.FS to Dirty.
RULE 34: The mstatus register is an MXLEN‑bit read/write register that tracks and controls the hart’s current operating state.
RULE 35: A restricted view of mstatus is provided as the sstatus register at the S‑level ISA.
RULE 36: In RV32 implementations, bit 31 of mstatus is the SD (state‑dirty) flag.
RULE 37: In RV32 implementations, bits 30‑25 of mstatus are WPRI fields that read as zero and ignore any writes.
RULE 38: In RV32 implementations, bit 24 of mstatus is the SDT flag.
RULE 39: In RV32 implementations, bit 23 of mstatus is the SPELP flag.
RULE 40: In RV32 implementations, bit 22 of mstatus is the TSR flag.
RULE 41: In RV32 implementations, bit 21 of mstatus is the TW flag.
RULE 42: In RV32 implementations, bit 20 of mstatus is the TVM flag.
RULE 43: In RV32 implementations, bit 19 of mstatus is the MXR flag.
RULE 44: In RV32 implementations, bit 18 of mstatus is the SUM flag.
RULE 45: In RV32 implementations, bit 17 of mstatus is the MPRV flag.
RULE 46: In RV32 implementations, bits 16‑15 of mstatus form the XS[1:0] field.
RULE 47: In RV32 implementations, bits 15‑14 of mstatus form the FS[1:0] field.
RULE 48: In RV32 implementations, bits 13‑12 of mstatus also form a second FS[1:0] field (duplicated for compatibility).
RULE 49: In RV32 implementations, bits 11‑10 of mstatus form the MPP[1:0] field.
RULE 50: In RV32 implementations, bits 9‑8 of mstatus form the VS[1:0] field.
RULE 51: In RV32 implementations, bit 7 of mstatus is an additional VS[1:0] bit.
RULE 52: In RV32 implementations, bit 6 of mstatus is the SPP flag.
RULE 53: In RV32 implementations, bit 5 of mstatus is the MPIE flag.
RULE 54: In RV32 implementations, bit 4 of mstatus is the UBE flag.
RULE 55: In RV32 implementations, bit 3 of mstatus is the SPIE flag.
RULE 56: In RV32 implementations, bit 2 of mstatus is a WPRI field that reads zero and ignores writes.
RULE 57: In RV32 implementations, bit 1 of mstatus is the MIE flag.
RULE 58: In RV32 implementations, bit 0 of mstatus is a WPRI field that reads zero and ignores writes.
RULE 59: In RV32 implementations, the lower half of mstatus includes an SIE flag located at bit 1 of that half.
RULE 60: In RV64 implementations, bits 63‑62 of mstatus are the SD flag (two‑bit encoding).
RULE 61: In RV64 implementations, bits 61‑60, 59‑58, 57‑56, 55‑54, 53‑52, and 51‑50 of mstatus are WPRI fields that read zero and ignore writes.
RULE 62: In RV64 implementations, bits 51‑50 of mstatus are the SDT flag.
RULE 63: In RV64 implementations, bits 49‑48 of mstatus are the SPELP flag.
RULE 64: In RV64 implementations, bits 47‑46 of mstatus are the TSR flag.
RULE 65: In RV64 implementations, bits 45‑44 of mstatus are the TW flag.
RULE 66: In RV64 implementations, bits 43‑42 of mstatus are the TVM flag.
RULE 67: In RV64 implementations, bits 41‑40 of mstatus are the MXR flag.
RULE 68: In RV64 implementations, bits 39‑38 of mstatus are the SUM flag.
RULE 69: In RV64 implementations, bits 37‑36 of mstatus are the MPRV flag.
RULE 70: In RV64 implementations, bits 35‑34 of mstatus form the XS[1:0] field.
RULE 71: In RV64 implementations, bits 33‑32 of mstatus form the FS[1:0] field.
RULE 72: In RV64 implementations, bits 31‑30 of mstatus form a second FS[1:0] field.
RULE 73: In RV64 implementations, bits 29‑28 of mstatus form the MPP[1:0] field.
RULE 74: In RV64 implementations, bits 27‑26 of mstatus form the VS[1:0] field.
RULE 75: In RV64 implementations, bits 25‑24 of mstatus form a second VS[1:0] field.
RULE 76: In RV64 implementations, bits 23‑22 of mstatus are the SPP flag.
RULE 77: In RV64 implementations, bits 21‑20 of mstatus are the MPIE flag.
RULE 78: In RV64 implementations, bits 19‑18 of mstatus are the UBE flag.
RULE 79: In RV64 implementations, bits 17‑16 of mstatus are the SPIE flag.
RULE 80: In RV64 implementations, bits 15‑14 of mstatus are WPRI fields that read zero and ignore writes.
RULE 81: In RV64 implementations, bits 13‑12 of mstatus are the MIE flag.
RULE 82: In RV64 implementations, bits 11‑10 of mstatus are WPRI fields that read zero and ignore writes.
RULE 83: In RV64 implementations, bits 9‑8 of mstatus are the SIE flag.
RULE 84: In RV64 implementations, bits 7‑0 of mstatus are WPRI fields that read zero and ignore writes.
RULE 85: For RV32 only, a separate register named mstatush exists as a 32‑bit read/write register.
RULE 86: In RV32, bits 30:4 of mstatush contain the same fields that appear in bits 62:36 of the RV64 mstatus register.
RULE 87: The mstatush register does not contain the SD, SXL, or UXL fields that are present in the full mstatus register.
RULE 88: The MIE and SIE bits reside in the low‑order bits of the mstatus CSR, so a single CSR instruction can atomically set or clear each of these bits.
RULE 89: When the hart is executing in privilege mode x (machine or supervisor), interrupts are globally enabled if and only if the corresponding xIE bit in mstatus is 1, and globally disabled if that bit is 0.
RULE 90: For any privilege mode w that is lower than the current execution mode x, interrupts are treated as globally disabled regardless of the wIE bit value in mstatus.
RULE 91: For any privilege mode y that is higher than the current execution mode x, interrupts are treated as globally enabled regardless of the yIE bit value in mstatus.
RULE 92: If supervisor mode is not implemented, the SIE and SPIE bits in mstatus are permanently 0 and read‑only.
RULE 93: For each privilege mode x that can respond to interrupts, mstatus contains a previous‑interrupt‑enable field xPIE that records the value of xIE before a trap.
RULE 94: For each privilege mode x that can respond to interrupts, mstatus contains a previous‑privilege‑mode field xPP that records the privilege mode in which the trap originated.
RULE 95: When a trap is taken from privilege mode y into privilege mode x, mstatus updates xPIE with the current value of xIE, sets xIE to 0, and stores y into xPP.
RULE 96: Executing an xRET instruction (MRET or SRET) restores interrupt enable by loading xIE from xPIE in mstatus.
RULE 97: Executing an xRET instruction changes the current privilege mode to the value stored in the xPP field of mstatus.
RULE 98: After an xRET instruction, mstatus sets the xPIE field to 1 to prepare for a possible nested trap.
RULE 99: After an xRET instruction, mstatus sets the xPP field to the least‑privileged supported mode (user mode if implemented, otherwise machine mode).
RULE 100: If the privilege mode restored by xRET is not machine mode, the xRET instruction also clears the MPRV bit in mstatus (sets MPRV to 0).
RULE 101: The xPP fields in mstatus are WARL: they may be written with any value, but reads return only a legal privilege mode (the mode x itself or any implemented lower mode).
RULE 102: If a privilege mode x is not implemented, the corresponding xPP field in mstatus is read‑only 0.
RULE 103: Machine‑mode software can test whether a particular privilege mode is implemented by writing the desired mode into the MPP field of mstatus and reading it back; a mismatch indicates the mode is absent.
RULE 104: When only user and machine privilege modes are implemented, the MPP field of mstatus can represent either user or machine mode using a single hardware storage bit.
RULE 105: After reset, the mstatus.MDT field is 1.
RULE 106: After reset, the value of the mstatus.MIE bit is implementation‑defined.
RULE 107: Writing a 1 to the mstatus.MDT field causes the mstatus.MIE bit to be cleared to 0.
RULE 108: On RV64, the clearing of the mstatus.MIE bit occurs unconditionally even if the same CSR write attempts to set mstatus.MIE to 1.
RULE 109: Writing a 0 to the mstatus.MDT field permits the mstatus.MIE bit to be set to 1 only if mstatus.MDT is already 0, or on RV64 only if the same CSR write also clears mstatus.MDT to 0.
RULE 110: When a trap is taken into M‑mode and mstatus.MDT is 0, hardware sets mstatus.MDT to 1 as part of the trap entry.
RULE 111: When a trap is taken into M‑mode and mstatus.MDT is 0, the hardware also clears mstatus.MIE to 0 as a consequence of mstatus.MDT becoming 1.
RULE 112: When a trap is taken into M‑mode and mstatus.MDT is already 1, the trap is classified as an unexpected double trap and the contents of mstatus remain unchanged.
RULE 113: The mstatus.MIE bit can be set to 1 only when mstatus.MDT is 0, or on RV64 when the same CSR write also clears mstatus.MDT to 0.
RULE 114: The mstatus.MIE bit cannot be set to 1 while mstatus.MDT is 1.
RULE 115: The mstatus.MIE bit is automatically cleared to 0 whenever the mstatus.MDT field is written with 1.
RULE 116: Executing MRET in M‑mode clears the mstatus.MDT field to 0.
RULE 117: Executing SRET in M‑mode clears the mstatus.MDT field to 0.
RULE 118: Executing MNRET clears the mstatus.MDT field to 0 if the privilege mode after the return is not M; otherwise mstatus.MDT is left unchanged.
RULE 119: Executing MRET, SRET, or MNRET has no direct effect on the mstatus.MIE bit.
RULE 120: In RV64 harts, the mstatus register contains SXL and UXL fields that are WARL and specify the XLEN used by S‑mode and U‑mode, using the same encoding as the MXL field of the misa register.
RULE 121: The value read from the SXL field of mstatus defines the effective XLEN for S‑mode, called SXLEN, and the value read from the UXL field defines the effective XLEN for U‑mode, called UXLEN.
RULE 122: When the implementation’s maximum XLEN (MXLEN) is 32, the mstatus register does not include a SXL field.
RULE 123: When MXLEN is 32, the mstatus register does not include a UXL field.
RULE 124: When MXLEN is 32, the effective SXLEN derived from mstatus is fixed at 32.
RULE 125: When MXLEN is 32, the effective UXLEN derived from mstatus is fixed at 32.
RULE 126: When MXLEN is 64 and the implementation does not support S‑mode, the SXL field in mstatus is a read‑only zero.
RULE 127: When MXLEN is 64 and the implementation supports S‑mode, the SXL field in mstatus is a WARL field that encodes the current SXLEN, and an implementation may choose to make it read‑only with a value that forces SXLEN to equal MXLEN.
RULE 128: When MXLEN is 64 and the implementation does not support U‑mode, the UXL field in mstatus is a read‑only zero.
RULE 129: When MXLEN is 64 and the implementation supports U‑mode, the UXL field in mstatus is a WARL field that encodes the current UXLEN, and an implementation may choose to make it read‑only with a value that forces UXLEN to equal MXLEN or to equal SXLEN.
RULE 130: If S‑mode is implemented, any write to the UXL field of mstatus to a value that would make UXLEN greater than SXLEN is illegal.
RULE 131: Whenever the XLEN for a privilege mode, as specified by the corresponding field in mstatus, is set to a value less than the implementation’s maximum XLEN, source‑operand register bits above that XLEN are ignored by all operations.
RULE 132: Whenever the XLEN for a privilege mode, as specified by the corresponding field in mstatus, is set to a value less than the implementation’s maximum XLEN, the result of an operation is sign‑extended to fill the full maximum XLEN in the destination register.
RULE 133: Whenever the XLEN for a privilege mode, as specified by the corresponding field in mstatus, is set to a value less than the implementation’s maximum XLEN, PC bits above that XLEN are ignored and any written PC value is sign‑extended to the maximum XLEN.
RULE 134: Implementations must ensure that every write to mstatus fully defines all bits of the underlying hardware register, eliminating any partially defined register contents.
RULE 135: The architecture imposes no checks on the values of the XLEN fields in mstatus to require that a lower‑privilege mode’s XLEN be less than or equal to the XLEN of the next higher privilege mode.
RULE 136: When a HINT instruction that would normally write back the same register value is executed while the current mode’s XLEN, as defined by mstatus, is less than MXLEN, and the upper bits of the destination register are not all equal to bit XLEN‑1, the implementation‑defined effect on those upper bits may be either to leave them unchanged or to overwrite them with copies of bit XLEN‑1.
RULE 137: The mstatus.MPRV bit controls the effective privilege mode for loads and stores only.
RULE 138: When the mstatus.MPRV bit is 0, loads and stores use the translation and protection mechanisms of the current privilege mode.
RULE 139: When the mstatus.MPRV bit is 1, load and store addresses are translated, protected, and have endianness applied as if the current privilege mode were the value in mstatus.MPP.
RULE 140: The mstatus.MPRV bit does not affect instruction address translation or protection.
RULE 141: The mstatus.MPRV bit is hard‑wired to 0 when U‑mode is not supported.
RULE 142: Executing an MRET or SRET that changes the privilege mode to a less‑privileged mode than M automatically clears the mstatus.MPRV bit to 0.
RULE 143: When the mstatus.MPRV bit is 1, the XLEN used for load and store addresses is treated as the XLEN of the privilege mode indicated by mstatus.MPP.
RULE 144: The mstatus.MXR bit modifies the privilege with which loads access virtual memory.
RULE 145: When the mstatus.MXR bit is 0, a load succeeds only from pages whose page‑table entry has the readable (R) bit set.
RULE 146: When the mstatus.MXR bit is 1, a load succeeds from pages whose page‑table entry has either the readable (R) bit or the executable (X) bit set.
RULE 147: The mstatus.MXR bit has no effect when page‑based virtual memory is not in effect.
RULE 148: The mstatus.MXR bit is hard‑wired to 0 when S‑mode is not supported.
RULE 149: The mstatus.SUM bit modifies the privilege with which S‑mode loads and stores access virtual memory.
RULE 150: When the mstatus.SUM bit is 0, S‑mode accesses to pages that are accessible by U‑mode (U=1 in the page‑table entry) cause a fault.
RULE 151: When the mstatus.SUM bit is 1, S‑mode accesses to pages that are accessible by U‑mode are permitted.
RULE 152: The mstatus.SUM bit has no effect when page‑based virtual memory is not in effect.
RULE 153: The mstatus.SUM bit is ignored when execution is not in S‑mode unless mstatus.MPRV is 1 and mstatus.MPP equals S, in which case it is in effect.
RULE 154: The mstatus.SUM bit is hard‑wired to 0 if S‑mode is not supported or if satp.MODE is read‑only 0.
RULE 155: The mstatus.MXR and mstatus.SUM bits affect only the interpretation of permission bits in page‑table entries.
RULE 156: The mstatus.MXR and mstatus.SUM bits do not influence the generation of access‑fault exceptions caused by PMA or PMP checks.
RULE 157: The bit named MBE resides in the mstatus register (and in mstatush for RV32‑E/64‑E) and is a WARL field whose hardware may restrict writable values to those defined as legal for the implementation.
RULE 158: When the M‑mode privilege bit mstatus.MPRV is zero, the value of mstatus.MBE determines the endianness of all non‑instruction‑fetch memory accesses performed in M‑mode.
RULE 159: If mstatus.MBE is zero, non‑instruction‑fetch accesses in M‑mode are little‑endian; if mstatus.MBE is one, those accesses are big‑endian.
RULE 160: Instruction fetches are little‑endian in all modes regardless of the value of mstatus.MBE.
RULE 161: The SBE bit exists in the mstatus register only on implementations that support S‑mode; otherwise it reads as zero and is read‑only.
RULE 162: The SBE field in mstatus is a WARL field that may be implemented as a read‑only copy of mstatus.MBE, but when writable it accepts only the values 0 or 1.
RULE 163: The value of mstatus.SBE controls the endianness of explicit load and store memory accesses executed in S‑mode.
RULE 164: When mstatus.SBE is zero, explicit S‑mode accesses are little‑endian; when it is one, they are big‑endian.
RULE 165: Implicit accesses to supervisor‑level memory‑management structures such as page tables always use the endianness specified by mstatus.SBE, independent of whether the access is explicit or implicit.
RULE 166: After any software changes the value of mstatus.SBE while supervisor‑level memory‑management data structures remain in use, M‑mode software must execute an SFENCE.VMA with rs1=x0 and rs2=x0.
RULE 167: The UBE bit is present in the mstatus register only on implementations that support U‑mode; otherwise it reads as zero and is read‑only.
RULE 168: The UBE field in mstatus is a WARL field that may be a read‑only copy of either mstatus.MBE or mstatus.SBE, but when writable it accepts only the values 0 or 1.
RULE 169: The value of mstatus.UBE determines the endianness of explicit load and store memory accesses performed in U‑mode.
RULE 170: When mstatus.UBE is zero, explicit U‑mode accesses are little‑endian; when it is one, they are big‑endian.
RULE 171: If an implementation supports only little‑endian memory accesses (aside from instruction fetches), the mstatus bits MBE, SBE, and UBE are all hard‑wired to zero and are read‑only.
RULE 172: If an implementation supports only big‑endian memory accesses (aside from instruction fetches), the mstatus bit MBE is hard‑wired to one and read‑only, and the mstatus bits SBE and UBE are hard‑wired to one and read‑only whenever S‑mode or U‑mode, respectively, are implemented.
RULE 173: All three endianness fields in mstatus (MBE, SBE, UBE) are WARL; a write of an illegal value may be silently rewritten to a legal value, and the architectural effect is as if the final legal value had been written.
RULE 174: An implementation that provides S‑mode may elect to make the mstatus.SBE field a read‑only alias of mstatus.MBE.
RULE 175: An implementation that provides U‑mode may elect to make the mstatus.UBE field a read‑only alias of either mstatus.MBE or mstatus.SBE.
RULE 176: Changing any of the endianness bits in the mstatus register, including mstatus.SBE, does not alter the little‑endian nature of instruction fetches in any privilege mode.
RULE 177: When mstatus.TVM is 1, any attempt in S-mode to read or write the satp CSR or to execute SFENCE.VMA or SINVAL.VMA raises an illegal-instruction exception.
RULE 178: When mstatus.TVM is 0, supervisor-mode accesses to the satp CSR and execution of SFENCE.VMA and SINVAL.VMA are permitted.
RULE 179: The TVM field in mstatus is read‑only zero when S-mode is not implemented.
RULE 180: When mstatus.TW is 0, the WFI instruction may execute in lower‑privilege modes (e.g., U-mode) provided it is not blocked for another reason.
RULE 181: When mstatus.TW is 1, execution of WFI in any less‑privileged mode that does not complete within an implementation‑specific bounded time limit causes an illegal-instruction exception.
RULE 182: When mstatus.TW is 1, an implementation may instead cause WFI to always raise an illegal‑instruction exception in less‑privileged modes, even if globally‑disabled interrupts are pending.
RULE 183: The TW field in mstatus is read‑only zero when no privilege modes exist that are less privileged than Machine mode.
RULE 184: When mstatus.TSR is 1, executing SRET while in S-mode raises an illegal-instruction exception.
RULE 185: When mstatus.TSR is 0, the SRET instruction is permitted in S-mode.
RULE 186: The TSR field in mstatus is read‑only zero when S-mode is not implemented.
RULE 187: The mstatus register contains the fields FS[1:0], VS[1:0], XS[1:0], and the read‑only SD bit that summarize extension context status.
RULE 188: The FS, VS, and XS fields of the mstatus register are WARL (write‑any‑legal‑value) unless an implementation makes them read‑only zero because the associated extension or S‑mode is not present.
RULE 189: The encoding 0, 1, 2, 3 for FS, VS, and XS in the mstatus register respectively mean Off, Initial, Clean, and Dirty.
RULE 190: The mstatus FS field must be readable‑writeable (non‑zero) when the F extension is implemented.
RULE 191: The mstatus FS field may be read‑only zero when neither the F extension nor S‑mode is implemented, or when S‑mode is present but the F extension is absent.
RULE 192: The mstatus VS field must be readable‑writeable (non‑zero) when vector registers are implemented.
RULE 193: The mstatus VS field may be read‑only zero when neither vector registers nor S‑mode are implemented, or when S‑mode is present but vectors are absent.
RULE 194: The mstatus XS field must be readable‑writeable (non‑zero) when any additional user‑mode extension that defines new state is implemented.
RULE 195: The mstatus XS field may be read‑only zero when no additional user extensions requiring new state are present.
RULE 196: An implementation may keep the mstatus FS or VS fields writable even if the corresponding extension is absent to allow emulation via traps into M‑mode.
RULE 197: The SD bit of the mstatus register is read‑only.
RULE 198: The SD bit of the mstatus register is 1 if and only if any of FS, VS, or XS in the mstatus register equals 0b11 (Dirty).
RULE 199: If all three of the mstatus fields FS, VS, and XS are read‑only zero, the SD bit of the mstatus register is always 0.
RULE 200: When the mstatus FS, VS, or XS field is Off, any instruction that reads or writes the corresponding unit’s state causes an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 201: When the mstatus FS, VS, or XS field is Initial, the corresponding unit’s state must behave as if it contains a defined constant initial value.
RULE 202: When the mstatus FS, VS, or XS field is Clean, the corresponding unit’s state may differ from the initial value but must match the last value saved during a context switch.
RULE 203: When the mstatus FS, VS, or XS field is Dirty, the corresponding unit’s state may have been modified since the last context save.
RULE 204: Changing the mstatus FS or VS field does not alter the contents of the floating‑point or vector registers, respectively.
RULE 205: During a privileged context save, if an mstatus field is Dirty the implementation must write the corresponding state to memory and then set that mstatus field to Clean.
RULE 206: During a privileged context save, if an mstatus field is Off, Initial, or Clean, the implementation must not write the corresponding state to memory.
RULE 207: During a privileged context restore, if an mstatus field is Clean the implementation must load the corresponding state from memory.
RULE 208: During a privileged context restore, if an mstatus field is Initial the implementation must set the corresponding state to its defined constant value without accessing memory.
RULE 209: During a privileged context restore, an mstatus field must never be Dirty.
RULE 210: During a privileged context restore, if an mstatus field is Off the implementation must take no action for the corresponding state.
RULE 211: Privileged code must read the mstatus FS, VS, and XS fields before performing a context save.
RULE 212: Privileged code may set the mstatus FS field directly when resuming a user context.
RULE 213: The mstatus XS field is set indirectly by writing the status register of each individual extension.
RULE 214: The mstatus FS, VS, and XS fields are also updated automatically by normal execution of instructions, independent of privilege mode.
RULE 215: An implementation may report a Dirty value in an mstatus field even when the corresponding state has not been modified.
RULE 216: Some implementations may support only the Off and Dirty values for an mstatus field, treating attempts to set Initial or Clean as Dirty.
RULE 217: Whether an instruction that writes a floating‑point or vector register without changing its contents causes a transition from Initial or Clean to Dirty in the mstatus register is implementation‑defined.
RULE 218: Platforms may forbid speculative writes that would set the mstatus FS field to Dirty in order to mitigate side‑channel leaks.
RULE 219: The Machine and Supervisor privilege modes share a single copy of the mstatus FS, VS, and XS bits.
RULE 220: Supervisor‑level software normally manipulates the mstatus FS, VS, and XS bits directly.
RULE 221: Machine‑level software must be conservative when saving or restoring extension state because it must honour the status observed by supervisor code via the mstatus register.
RULE 222: The Zicfilp extension adds the Supervisor‑mode Previous Expected Landing Pad (SPELP) field to the mstatus register.
RULE 223: The Zicfilp extension adds the Machine‑mode Previous Expected Landing Pad (MPELP) field to the mstatus register.
RULE 224: The SPELP field of mstatus records the previous Expected Landing Pad state.
RULE 225: The MPELP field of mstatus records the previous Expected Landing Pad state.
RULE 226: The SPELP field of mstatus is updated whenever the Expected Landing Pad state changes according to the architectural rules for Expected Landing Pad handling.
RULE 227: The MPELP field of mstatus is updated whenever the Expected Landing Pad state changes according to the architectural rules for Expected Landing Pad handling.
RULE 228: An encoding value of 0 in the SPELP field of mstatus signifies NO_LP_EXPECTED, meaning no landing pad instruction is expected.
RULE 229: An encoding value of 1 in the SPELP field of mstatus signifies LP_EXPECTED, meaning a landing pad instruction is expected.
RULE 230: An encoding value of 0 in the MPELP field of mstatus signifies NO_LP_EXPECTED, meaning no landing pad instruction is expected.
RULE 231: An encoding value of 1 in the MPELP field of mstatus signifies LP_EXPECTED, meaning a landing pad instruction is expected.
RULE 232: When a trap is delegated to S‑mode, the SPP field of mstatus is written with the privilege mode that was active at the time the trap occurred.
RULE 233: When a trap is delegated to S‑mode, the SPIE field of mstatus is written with the value that the SIE field held at the time the trap occurred.
RULE 234: When a trap is delegated to S‑mode, the SIE field of mstatus is cleared to 0.
RULE 235: When a trap is delegated to S‑mode, the MPP field of mstatus is not written and therefore retains its previous value.
RULE 236: When a trap is delegated to S‑mode, the MPIE field of mstatus is not written and therefore retains its previous value.
RULE 237: The mstatus register defines a Machine Interrupt Enable (MIE) bit that indicates whether machine‑mode interrupts are globally enabled.
RULE 238: If the current privilege mode is Machine and the MIE bit in mstatus is set, a pending interrupt i causes a trap to Machine mode.
RULE 239: If the current privilege mode is less privileged than Machine mode, a pending interrupt i causes a trap to Machine mode regardless of the MIE bit in mstatus.
RULE 240: After execution of any xRET instruction, the hardware must immediately re‑evaluate interrupt‑trap conditions that depend on the MIE bit in mstatus.
RULE 241: After an explicit write to any CSR on which interrupt‑trap conditions depend, including mstatus, the hardware must immediately re‑evaluate those interrupt‑trap conditions.
RULE 242: The mstatus register includes a TSR (Trap SRET) field.
RULE 243: If the TSR bit in mstatus is set to 1, execution of the SRET instruction shall raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 244: If the TW bit in mstatus is set to 1, execution of the WFI instruction must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 245: The operation of the WFI instruction is unaffected by the values of the MIE and SIE bits in mstatus.
RULE 246: After executing WFI, the hart must resume execution when a locally enabled interrupt becomes pending, regardless of the values of MIE or SIE in mstatus.
RULE 247: Upon reset, the MIE field in the mstatus register is cleared to 0.
RULE 248: Upon reset, the MPRV field in the mstatus register is cleared to 0.
RULE 249: When the implementation supports little‑endian memory accesses, the MBE field in the mstatus (or its high half mstatush) is cleared to 0.
RULE 250: No WARL field, including any WARL fields in the mstatus register, may ever hold an illegal value.
