RULE 251: The mstatus register contains the MPRV bit, which controls whether memory accesses are performed with a different effective privilege mode.
RULE 252: The mstatus register contains the MPP field, which records the previous privilege mode and can hold the values M, S, or U.
RULE 253: PMP checks are applied to data accesses that occur while the core is in M‑mode only if the MPRV bit in mstatus is set and the MPP field in mstatus holds either S or U.
RULE 254: If either the MPRV bit in mstatus is cleared or the MPP field in mstatus contains M, PMP checks are not applied to data accesses in M‑mode.
RULE 255: When the MPRV bit in mstatus is set and the MPP field in mstatus holds S or U, the effective privilege mode for that data access is treated as the mode indicated by the MPP field.
RULE 256: PMP checks are performed according to the effective privilege mode derived from the MPP field in mstatus when the MPRV bit is set and MPP is S or U.
RULE 257: The mstatus register may be extended with an XS field for each relevant extension to control the use of new user-level CSRs, analogous to the VS field used for the vector extension.
RULE 258: Adding an XS field to mstatus is intended to prevent new user‑level CSRs from being used as covert channels.
RULE 259: The approach of adding multiple XS fields to mstatus is not considered a general solution because the status registers contain a limited number of bits that cannot accommodate all future extensions.
RULE 260: When executing in M‑mode, setting the mstatus.MPRV field allows M‑mode to read from or write to regions that are marked S‑mode or U‑mode only, causing those accesses to be performed with S/U‑mode permissions.
RULE 261: When the mstatus.MPRV bit is set, memory accesses are subject to the S‑mode MMU restrictions that apply to S/U‑mode permissions.
RULE 262: The architecture shall not provide a mechanism to permanently disable the mstatus.MPRV bit, as the bit must remain functional.
RULE 263: The MIE field in mstatus determines whether ordinary machine‑mode interrupts are enabled.
RULE 264: RNMI signals are not disabled by clearing mstatus.MIE; they remain enabled regardless of the value of that field.
RULE 265: The encoding of the MPP field in mstatus must use the same numeric values and meanings as the MNPP field defined for the mnstatus CSR.
RULE 266: The encoding of the MPV field in mstatus must be identical to the encoding used for the MNPV field defined for the mnstatus CSR.
RULE 267: If the NMIE bit is zero, the hardware must treat mstatus.MPRV as if it were cleared, regardless of the actual value stored in mstatus.MPRV.
RULE 268: When the MNRET instruction changes the current privilege mode to a mode that is less privileged than Machine mode, the MPRV field in the mstatus register is cleared to 0.
RULE 269: The mstatus register contains every architectural field defined in the sstatus register, so the set of sstatus fields is a subset of the fields present in mstatus.
RULE 270: A read or write of any field via the sstatus CSR accesses the identical bits in mstatus, causing the value read or written to be exactly the same as if the same field were accessed directly in mstatus.
RULE 271: When the V bit in the sstatus register is 0, any read or write of the SPELP field accesses the homonymous field in the mstatus register.
RULE 272: When the V bit in the sstatus register is 1, reads or writes of the SPELP field do not access the mstatus register but instead access the homonymous field in the vsstatus register.
RULE 273: The mstatus register contains a field named SUM.
RULE 274: The mstatus register contains a field named MXR.
RULE 275: The virtual‑address translation algorithm uses the value of the SUM field in mstatus, together with the current privilege mode and the MXR field in mstatus, to decide whether a memory access complies with the pte.u bit.
RULE 276: If the combination of the current privilege mode, the SUM field in mstatus, and the MXR field in mstatus indicates that a memory access is not permitted with respect to the pte.u bit, the algorithm must raise a page‑fault exception of the same type as the original access.
RULE 277: If mstatus.TVM = 1, an attempt to execute SINVAL.VMA while the processor is in S-mode or HS-mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 278: If mstatus.TVM = 1, an attempt to execute HINVAL.GVMA while the processor is in S-mode or HS-mode must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 279: SFENCE.W.INVAL is always permitted in S-mode regardless of the value of mstatus.TVM.
RULE 280: SFENCE.W.INVAL is always permitted in VS-mode regardless of the value of mstatus.TVM.
RULE 281: SFENCE.INVAL.IR is always permitted in S-mode regardless of the value of mstatus.TVM.
RULE 282: SFENCE.INVAL.IR is always permitted in VS-mode regardless of the value of mstatus.TVM.
RULE 283: When mstatus.TVM = 1, execution of SFENCE.W.INVAL does not need to be trapped because it has only ordering effects and no visible architectural side effects.
RULE 284: When mstatus.TVM = 1, execution of SFENCE.INVAL.IR does not need to be trapped because it has only ordering effects and no visible architectural side effects.
RULE 285: The mstatus CSR contains the fields TSR, TW, and TVM, which affect execution in regular (non‑VS) mode and raise illegal‑instruction exceptions rather than virtual‑instruction exceptions.
RULE 286: When mstatus.TSR is set to 1, any attempt to execute the SRET instruction in a privilege mode where SRET is not permitted raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 287: When mstatus.TW is set to 1, any attempt to execute the WFI instruction that does not complete within an implementation‑specific bounded time limit raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 288: When mstatus.TVM is set to 1, any attempt to execute the SFENCE.VMA instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 289: When mstatus.TVM is set to 1, any attempt to execute the SINVAL.VMA instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 290: When mstatus.TVM is set to 1, any access to the satp CSR raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 291: If the mstatus.TV M field is 1, any attempt to read from or write to the hgatp CSR while executing in HS‑mode must cause an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 292: The mstatus register includes a Modify Privilege (MPRV) bit that can be set to enable the MPRV feature.
RULE 293: When the virtual‑machine mode bit V in vsstatus is zero, the mstatus register does not cause vsstatus to affect machine behavior unless its MPRV feature is enabled.
RULE 294: If the MPRV bit in mstatus is set while V=0, any load or store instruction is executed as though V were one.
RULE 295: When the MPRV bit in mstatus is set, any load or store is performed as if the V bit in mstatus were 1.
RULE 296: The effect of the MPRV bit in mstatus applies even when the V bit in mstatus is 0.
RULE 297: The MPRV feature in mstatus enables virtual‑machine address translation for memory accesses while the actual V mode is disabled.
RULE 298: The mstatus CSR contains a field named TVM.
RULE 299: When the processor is in HS-mode and mstatus.TVM = 0, execution of HFENCE.GVMA is permitted.
RULE 300: When the processor is in HS-mode and mstatus.TVM = 1, execution of HFENCE.GVMA raises an illegal-instruction exception.
RULE 301: In M-mode the value of mstatus.TVM is ignored and HFENCE.GVMA is always permitted.
RULE 302: The value of mstatus.TVM does not cause an exception when executing HFENCE.VVMA.
RULE 303: The presence of the hypervisor extension causes the machine status register mstatus to contain additional hypervisor‑defined fields.
RULE 304: The presence of the hypervisor extension may change the semantics of existing bits in the machine status register mstatus.
RULE 305: In a 64‑bit implementation (MXLEN=64) the MPV and GVA fields are part of the mstatus CSR, while in a 32‑bit implementation they are located in the mstatush CSR.
RULE 306: The implementation writes the MPV bit in mstatus whenever a trap is taken into M‑mode, setting it to the value of the current virtualization mode V at the moment of the trap.
RULE 307: When an MRET instruction executes, the virtualization mode V is restored from the MPV field of mstatus unless the MPP field of mstatus equals 3, in which case V is forced to 0.
RULE 308: The implementation writes the GVA field in mstatus whenever a trap is taken into M‑mode.
RULE 309: If a trap that writes a guest virtual address to mtval (breakpoint, address‑misaligned, access‑fault, page‑fault, or guest‑page‑fault) occurs, the GVA field of mstatus is set to 1.
RULE 310: For any trap into M‑mode that does not write a guest virtual address to mtval, the GVA field of mstatus is set to 0.
RULE 311: The TSR field of mstatus affects execution only when the processor is in HS‑mode and has no effect in VS‑mode.
RULE 312: The TVM field of mstatus affects execution only when the processor is in HS‑mode and has no effect in VS‑mode.
RULE 313: The TW field of mstatus influences execution in all privilege modes except M‑mode.
RULE 314: When the TVM bit in mstatus is 1, HS‑mode is prohibited from accessing hgatp and from executing HFENCE.GVMA or HINVAL.GVMA, but accesses to vsatp and execution of HFENCE.VVMA or HINVAL.VVMA remain permitted.
RULE 315: When the MPRV field in mstatus is 0, memory translation and protection behave normally using the current privilege mode.
RULE 316: When the MPRV field in mstatus is 1, explicit memory accesses are translated, protected, and have endianness applied as if the current virtualization mode were the value of the MPV field of mstatus and the nominal privilege mode were the value of the MPP field of mstatus.
RULE 317: If MPRV=1, MPV=0, and MPP=0 in mstatus, the access is performed as a U‑level access using only HS‑level translation and protection.
RULE 318: If MPRV=1, MPV=0, and MPP=1 in mstatus, the access is performed as an HS‑level access using only HS‑level translation and protection.
RULE 319: If MPRV=1 and MPP=3 in mstatus (MPV is ignored), the access is performed as an M‑level access with no translation.
RULE 320: If MPRV=1, MPV=1, and MPP=0 in mstatus, the access is performed as a VU‑level access with two‑stage translation and protection.
RULE 321: If MPRV=1, MPV=1, and MPP=1 in mstatus, the access is performed as a VS‑level access with two‑stage translation and protection.
RULE 322: The MPRV setting in mstatus does not affect the virtual‑machine load/store instructions HLV, HLVX, and HSV; for those instructions, explicit loads and stores always behave as though V=1 and the nominal privilege mode equals hstatus.SPVP, overriding any MPRV value.
RULE 323: The mstatus register is defined as a superset of the HS‑level sstatus register.
RULE 324: The mstatus register is not a superset of the VS‑level vsstatus register.
RULE 325: In VS-mode, the values of mstatus.TSR and mstatus.TVM have no effect on the detection of any exceptions.
RULE 326: When evaluating whether an instruction is HS‑qualified, the test assumes that mstatus.TSR equals zero and mstatus.TVM equals zero.
RULE 327: In VS-mode or VU-mode, an access to an implemented non‑high‑half hypervisor CSR or VS CSR that would be permitted in HS-mode assuming mstatus.TVM equals zero causes a virtual‑instruction exception if mstatus.TVM is not zero.
RULE 328: In VS-mode or VU-mode with XLEN equal to 32, an access to an implemented high‑half hypervisor CSR or high‑half VS CSR that would be permitted in HS-mode assuming mstatus.TVM equals zero causes a virtual‑instruction exception if mstatus.TVM is not zero.
RULE 329: In VU-mode, attempting to execute the WFI instruction while mstatus.TW equals zero triggers a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 330: In VU-mode, attempting to execute a supervisor instruction such as SRET or SFENCE triggers a virtual‑instruction exception regardless of the value of mstatus.TW.
RULE 331: In VS-mode, when hstatus.VTW equals one, attempting to execute WFI while mstatus.TW equals zero triggers a virtual‑instruction exception unless the instruction completes within an implementation‑specific bounded time.
RULE 332: The bits mstatus.TSR, mstatus.TVM, and mstatus.TW together determine whether certain privileged instructions and CSR accesses are permitted or cause a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 333: The bits mstatus.TSR and mstatus.TVM influence exception behavior only in S‑mode and have no normative effect in VS-mode.
RULE 334: When a trap is taken into M‑mode, the V field in mstatus is cleared to 0.
RULE 335: When a trap is taken into M‑mode, the MPV and MPP fields in mstatus (or mstatush) are written according to the previous privilege mode: U‑mode writes MPV=0, MPP=0; HS‑mode writes MPV=0, MPP=1; M‑mode writes MPV=0, MPP=3; VU‑mode writes MPV=1, MPP=0; VS‑mode writes MPV=1, MPP=1.
RULE 336: When a trap is taken into M‑mode, the GVA, MPIE, and MIE fields in mstatus (or mstatush) are written as part of the trap entry.
RULE 337: During execution of the MRET instruction, if the MPP field in mstatus equals 3, the MPV field in mstatus is ignored.
RULE 338: The mstatus (or mstatush) register contains the fields MPP, MPV, MIE, and MPIE that are used by the MRET instruction.
RULE 339: When the MRET instruction is executed, it determines the new privilege mode from the current values of MPP and MPV in mstatus (or mstatush).
RULE 340: After determining the new privilege mode, MRET clears the MPV field of mstatus (or mstatush) to 0.
RULE 341: After determining the new privilege mode, MRET clears the MPP field of mstatus (or mstatush) to 0.
RULE 342: After determining the new privilege mode, MRET sets the MIE field of mstatus (or mstatush) to the previous value of MPIE.
RULE 343: After determining the new privilege mode, MRET sets the MPIE field of mstatus (or mstatush) to 1.
RULE 344: The remainder of the MRET operation, including setting the privilege mode and the program counter, is performed after these updates to the fields of mstatus (or mstatush).
RULE 345: The mstatus CSR contains a single MPELP bit that stores the previous Expected Landing Pad (ELP) state when a trap is delivered to M‑mode.
RULE 346: The mstatus CSR contains a single SPELP bit that stores the previous Expected Landing Pad (ELP) state when a trap is delivered to S‑mode or HS‑mode, and this bit can be read and written via the sstatus CSR with identical architectural effect.
RULE 347: When a trap is taken into privilege mode x (where x is M, S, or HS), the corresponding x‑PELP bit in mstatus (MPELP for M‑mode, SPELP for S/HS‑mode) is set to the current value of the architectural ELP field.
RULE 348: After the x‑PELP bit in mstatus is set during trap entry, the architectural ELP field is set to the constant value NO_LP_EXPECTED.
RULE 349: When an xRET instruction (such as MRET or SRET) is executed, the architectural ELP field is set to the value of the x‑PELP bit in mstatus if the target privilege mode y has its LPE field equal to 1; otherwise the ELP field is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED.
RULE 350: Following the ELP update on an xRET, the x‑PELP bit in mstatus (MPELP for MRET, SPELP for SRET) is cleared to NO_LP_EXPECTED.
RULE 351: The coordinated handling of the MPELP and SPELP bits in mstatus preserves ELP state across traps and returns, thereby implementing correct expected landing‑pad semantics.
RULE 352: In M-mode, accessing mstatus register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 353: In S-mode, accessing mstatus register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 354: In U-mode, accessing mstatus register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 355: In M-mode, setting mstatus.SD (bit 63) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000].
RULE 356: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MDT (bit 42) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000000000].
RULE 357: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MPV (bit 39) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000].
RULE 358: In M-mode, setting mstatus.GVA (bit 38) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000000000].
RULE 359: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MBE (bit 37) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000000000].
RULE 360: In M-mode, setting mstatus.SBE (bit 36) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000000].
RULE 361: In M-mode, setting mstatus.SXL (bit 34) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400000000].
RULE 362: In M-mode, setting mstatus.UXL (bit 32) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000000].
RULE 363: In M-mode, setting mstatus.TSR (bit 22) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400000].
RULE 364: In M-mode, setting mstatus.TW (bit 21) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200000].
RULE 365: In M-mode, setting mstatus.TVM (bit 20) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000].
RULE 366: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MXR (bit 19) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000].
RULE 367: In M-mode, setting mstatus.SUM (bit 18) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000].
RULE 368: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MPRV (bit 17) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20000].
RULE 369: In M-mode, setting mstatus.XS (bit 15) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000].
RULE 370: In M-mode, setting mstatus.FS (bit 13) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000].
RULE 371: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MPP (bit 11) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800].
RULE 372: In M-mode, setting mstatus.VS (bit 9) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200].
RULE 373: In M-mode, setting mstatus.SPP (bit 8) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100].
RULE 374: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MPIE (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 375: In M-mode, setting mstatus.UBE (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 376: In M-mode, setting mstatus.SPIE (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 377: In M-mode, setting mstatus.MIE (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 378: In M-mode, setting mstatus.SIE (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 379: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mstatus register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x300, a0; csrr a1, 0x300; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 380: In M-mode, accessing mstatus register while F extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: F; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 381: In M-mode, accessing mstatus register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 382: In M-mode, accessing mstatus register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 383: In M-mode, accessing mstatus register while Zfinx extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zfinx; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 384: In M-mode, accessing mstatus register while Zicfilp extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicfilp; csrr a0, 0x300; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mtvec
RULE 1: The mtvec register is a machine‑mode CSR whose width equals XLEN bits and whose value is WARL when the register is writable.
RULE 2: The mtvec CSR must be present in every implementation, although it may be read‑only in some implementations.
RULE 3: When writable, the set of values that can be written to mtvec is implementation‑defined (WARL).
RULE 4: The BASE field of mtvec must be aligned on a 4‑byte boundary, and the CSR stores only bits XLEN‑1 down to 2 of the address, with the lower two bits forced to zero.
RULE 5: The MODE field of mtvec selects the trap‑vector mode using the encoding 0 = Direct, 1 = Vectored, and values ≥2 are reserved.
RULE 6: When mtvec.MODE = Direct, every trap that transfers control to machine mode sets the program counter to the address contained in mtvec.BASE.
RULE 7: When mtvec.MODE = Vectored, a synchronous exception sets the program counter to mtvec.BASE.
RULE 8: When mtvec.MODE = Vectored, an asynchronous interrupt sets the program counter to mtvec.BASE plus four times the interrupt cause number.
RULE 9: In mtvec.MODE = Vectored, the address used for an interrupt with cause number N is calculated as mtvec.BASE + 4·N (for example, cause 7 yields mtvec.BASE + 0x1C).
RULE 10: An implementation may impose additional alignment constraints on mtvec.BASE beyond the mandatory 4‑byte alignment, and these constraints may differ for different mtvec.MODE values.
RULE 11: Implementations may require stricter alignment of mtvec.BASE when mtvec.MODE = Vectored than when mtvec.MODE = Direct.
RULE 12: The locations of the reset and non‑maskable‑interrupt vectors are defined by the platform specification and are not determined by the mtvec register.
RULE 13: In M-mode, accessing mtvec register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x305; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 14: In S-mode, accessing mtvec register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x305; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 15: In U-mode, accessing mtvec register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x305; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 16: In M-mode, setting mtvec.BASE (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x305, a0; csrr a1, 0x305; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 17: In M-mode, setting mtvec.MODE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x305, a0; csrr a1, 0x305; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 18: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mtvec register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x305, a0; csrr a1, 0x305; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].

REGISTER: pmpaddr0
RULE 1: pmpaddr0 supplies the address portion of PMP entry 0.
RULE 2: In RV32 implementations, the value stored in pmpaddr0 encodes bits 33‑2 of a 34‑bit physical address.
RULE 3: In RV64 implementations, the value stored in pmpaddr0 encodes bits 55‑2 of a 56‑bit physical address.
RULE 4: The architectural width of pmpaddr0 equals the current hart's MXLEN, being 32 bits on RV32 and 64 bits on RV64.
RULE 5: Writes to pmpaddr0 are WARL, meaning any value may be written but the value read back is a legal value possibly masked to the implemented address bits.
RULE 6: pmpaddr0 may be hard‑wired to zero, in which case reads always return zero.
RULE 7: Reads and writes of pmpaddr0 are permitted only when the hart is executing in machine mode.
RULE 8: pmpaddr0 is present only if the implementation provides at least one PMP entry; implementations may provide zero, 16, or 64 entries, with the lowest‑numbered entries implemented first.
RULE 9: In TOR mode for PMP entry 0, the address range matched by pmpaddr0 is the half‑open interval from zero up to (but not including) the value stored in pmpaddr0, i.e., any address y satisfies y < pmpaddr0.
RULE 10: When the software writes the maximum XLEN‑wide value (2^XLEN‑1) to pmpaddr0 and then reads it back, the index of the least‑significant bit that reads as 1 is defined as G, which is used to compute the PMP granularity.
RULE 11: If the processor XLEN exceeds the implementation‑defined MXLEN, the value of pmpaddr0 is zero‑extended from MXLEN bits to XLEN bits before it participates in address‑matching.
RULE 12: When pmpcfg0.A is OFF or TOR, reads of pmpaddr0 return zeros in bits [G‑1:0] regardless of the stored value.
RULE 13: When pmpcfg0.A is NAPOT, reads of pmpaddr0 return ones in bits [G‑2:0] regardless of the stored value.
RULE 14: The read‑mask applied to pmpaddr0 does not modify the actual stored bits; the original contents of pmpaddr0 are preserved when the A field changes between NAPOT and OFF/TOR.
RULE 15: In TOR mode, the address‑matching logic ignores bits [G‑1:0] of pmpaddr0; only the upper bits above G‑1 are used to determine the range boundary.
RULE 16: The PMP granularity computed from the least‑significant 1 bit of the value read from pmpaddr0 equals 2^(G+2) bytes.
RULE 17: In M-mode, accessing pmpaddr0 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x3b0; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 18: In S-mode, accessing pmpaddr0 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x3b0; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 19: In U-mode, accessing pmpaddr0 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x3b0; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 20: In M-mode, setting pmpaddr0.ADDR (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x3b0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3b0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 21: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the pmpaddr0 register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x3b0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3b0; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].

REGISTER: pmpcfg0
RULE 1: pmpcfg0 is a Machine‑mode CSR that holds PMP configuration fields.
RULE 2: On RV32 implementations pmpcfg0 is 32 bits wide and contains four 8‑bit PMP configuration fields for entries 0 through 3.
RULE 3: On RV64 implementations pmpcfg0 is 64 bits wide and contains eight 8‑bit PMP configuration fields for entries 0 through 7.
RULE 4: pmpcfg0 is a legal CSR on both RV32 and RV64 implementations.
RULE 5: All PMP CSRs, including pmpcfg0, are accessible only while the processor is executing in Machine mode.
RULE 6: All fields inside pmpcfg0 are WARL, meaning any written value that is legal for the implementation may be accepted and any unimplemented field may read back as zero.
RULE 7: In each 8‑bit slot of pmpcfg0 bit 0 is the R bit, which when set permits read accesses for the corresponding PMP entry.
RULE 8: In each 8‑bit slot of pmpcfg0 bit 1 is the W bit, which when set permits write accesses for the corresponding PMP entry.
RULE 9: In each 8‑bit slot of pmpcfg0 bit 2 is the X bit, which when set permits instruction‑fetch accesses for the corresponding PMP entry.
RULE 10: In each 8‑bit slot of pmpcfg0 bits 3‑4 are the A field that selects the address‑matching mode for the corresponding PMP entry.
RULE 11: In each 8‑bit slot of pmpcfg0 bit 7 is the L (lock) bit that, when set, locks the corresponding PMP entry configuration.
RULE 12: In each 8‑bit slot of pmpcfg0 bits 5‑6 are reserved and must be zero.
RULE 13: The R, W, and X bits together form a WARL subfield in each slot of pmpcfg0, and the combination R = 0 and W = 1 is reserved and therefore illegal to write.
RULE 14: If an instruction fetch targets a physical address within a PMP region whose corresponding slot in pmpcfg0 has X = 0, an instruction access‑fault exception is generated.
RULE 15: If a load or load‑reserved instruction accesses a physical address within a PMP region whose corresponding slot in pmpcfg0 has R = 0, a load access‑fault exception is generated.
RULE 16: If a store, store‑conditional, or AMO instruction accesses a physical address within a PMP region whose corresponding slot in pmpcfg0 has W = 0, a store access‑fault exception is generated.
RULE 17: The permission consequences described for the R, W, and X bits apply to every PMP entry configured by pmpcfg0, i.e., entries 0‑3 on RV32 and entries 0‑7 on RV64.
RULE 18: On RV64 implementations the odd‑numbered pmpcfg CSRs are illegal, while pmpcfg0, being even‑numbered, is legal.
RULE 19: In pmpcfg0 the 2‑bit A field value 0 (OFF) disables entry 0 so that it matches no addresses.
RULE 20: In pmpcfg0 the 2‑bit A field value 1 (TOR) sets the lower bound of entry 0 to 0 and makes entry 0 match any address y that is less than pmpaddr0.
RULE 21: In pmpcfg0 the 2‑bit A field value 2 (NA4) configures entry 0 to match a naturally aligned 4‑byte region.
RULE 22: In pmpcfg0 the 2‑bit A field value 3 (NAPOT) configures entry 0 to match a naturally aligned power‑of‑two region of size at least 8 bytes, with the size encoded in the low bits of pmpaddr0.
RULE 23: When pmpcfg0.A[1] equals 1 (NAPOT mode), bits [G‑2:0] of pmpaddr0 read back as all 1’s.
RULE 24: When pmpcfg0.A[1] equals 0 (OFF or TOR mode), bits [G‑1:0] of pmpaddr0 read back as all 0’s.
RULE 25: The read‑back values of the low bits of pmpaddr0 do not affect the address‑matching logic for TOR mode.
RULE 26: Changing pmpcfg0.A from NAPOT to OFF or TOR and later back to NAPOT preserves the original values of bits [G‑1:0] in pmpaddr0.
RULE 27: Writing zero to pmpcfg0 clears the A field to OFF, which forces the address register to be interpreted in OFF/TOR mode for subsequent reads.
RULE 28: After writing zero to pmpcfg0 and then writing all‑ones to pmpaddr0, reading back pmpaddr0 reveals a least‑significant set bit whose index equals the granularity exponent G.
RULE 29: The algorithm that writes zero to pmpcfg0, writes all‑ones to pmpaddr0, and reads back pmpaddr0 is guaranteed to determine the granularity G because the zero write causes low‑order bits to read as zeros while the all‑ones write exposes the granularity‑dependent mask.
RULE 30: When XLEN exceeds MXLEN, the hardware zero‑extends pmpaddr0 from MXLEN to XLEN bits for address‑matching, without changing pmpcfg0, thereby affecting the effective address range matched by entry 0.
RULE 31: In M-mode, accessing pmpcfg0 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x3a0; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 32: In S-mode, accessing pmpcfg0 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x3a0; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 33: In U-mode, accessing pmpcfg0 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x3a0; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 34: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp0cfg (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 35: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp1cfg (bit 8) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100].
RULE 36: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp2cfg (bit 16) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000].
RULE 37: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp3cfg (bit 24) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000].
RULE 38: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp4cfg (bit 32) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000000; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000000].
RULE 39: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp5cfg (bit 40) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000000000; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000000000].
RULE 40: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp6cfg (bit 48) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000000000; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000000000].
RULE 41: In M-mode, setting pmpcfg0.pmp7cfg (bit 56) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000000000000; csrs 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000000000000].
RULE 42: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the pmpcfg0 register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x3a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x3a0; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].

REGISTER: satp
RULE 1: Implementations may perform implicit reads of the satp CSR.
RULE 2: All S‑mode instruction fetches implicitly read the satp CSR.
RULE 3: The value returned by an implicit read of satp is identical to the value that would be returned by an explicit read of satp using a CSR‑access instruction executed in a privilege mode that is sufficient to read the register.
RULE 4: If S‑mode is not supported, the SUM bit in satp is read‑only 0.
RULE 5: If the MODE field of satp is read‑only 0, the SUM bit in satp is read‑only 0.
RULE 6: If the TVM bit in mstatus is 1 and the processor is executing in S‑mode, any attempt to read or write the satp CSR must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 7: If the TVM bit in mstatus is 0, reads and writes of the satp CSR are permitted in S‑mode and must not raise an exception.
RULE 8: When S‑mode is not supported, the TVM field is a WARL field that is read‑only zero, which means accesses to satp are never prohibited by TVM.
RULE 9: The satp register includes a MODE field that determines which address‑translation mode the processor uses.
RULE 10: If an implementation forces satp.MODE to read‑only zero, the implementation may also force the menvcfg.FIOM bit to read‑only zero.
RULE 11: When satp.MODE is permanently zero, the processor must operate in Bare mode for all address translations.
RULE 12: The satp register provides the physical address of the root of the page‑table hierarchy used for address translation.
RULE 13: The PBMTE bit in menvcfg governs the availability of the Svpbmt extension for translations that use a root physical address supplied by satp.
RULE 14: When satp.MODE is 0, the SUM bit in sstatus is read‑only and always reads as 0.
RULE 15: Write permission to the SUM bit in sstatus is available only if satp.MODE is not 0.
RULE 16: If the satp.MODE field is read‑only zero, then the implementation may also make the FIOM bit in senvcfg read‑only zero.
RULE 17: satp is an SXLEN‑bit read/write control‑status register that controls supervisor‑mode address translation and protection.
RULE 18: The PPN field of satp holds the physical page number of the root page table, defined as the supervisor physical address of the page‑table base divided by 4 KiB.
RULE 19: Storing a PPN rather than a full address in satp permits a physical address space larger than 4 GiB on RV32 implementations.
RULE 20: Implementations may impose constraints on the values that the satp.PPN field can contain, and some platforms require that all physical page numbers for main memory be representable.
RULE 21: The ASID field of satp provides an address‑space identifier that enables address‑translation fences on a per‑address‑space basis.
RULE 22: The number of implemented ASID bits, called ASIDLEN, is implementation‑defined and may be zero.
RULE 23: The implemented ASID bits of satp are discovered by writing 1 to every bit of the ASID field and reading back which bits retain a 1, with the least‑significant bits implemented first.
RULE 24: If ASIDLEN > 0 then the bits satp.ASID[ASIDLEN‑1:0] are writable.
RULE 25: The maximal ASIDLEN allowed by the architecture is 9 for Sv32 and 16 for Sv39, Sv48, and Sv57.
RULE 26: The MODE field of satp selects the current supervisor address‑translation scheme.
RULE 27: When satp.MODE is set to Bare, supervisor virtual addresses are identical to supervisor physical addresses and no additional memory‑protection beyond the physical‑memory scheme is provided.
RULE 28: To select satp.MODE = Bare, software must write zero to all other bits of satp (bits 30‑0 for SXLEN = 32 and bits 59‑0 for SXLEN = 64).
RULE 29: Writing a non‑zero pattern to the non‑MODE bits of satp while selecting Bare causes unspecified effects on the remaining fields and on address‑translation or protection behavior.
RULE 30: For SXLEN = 32, encodings with satp.MODE = Bare and satp.ASID[8:7] = 3 are designated for custom use.
RULE 31: For SXLEN = 32, encodings with satp.MODE = Bare and satp.ASID[8:7] ≠ 3 are reserved for future standard use.
RULE 32: For SXLEN = 64, all encodings with satp.MODE = Bare are reserved for future standard use.
RULE 33: For SXLEN = 32 the only defined non‑Bare satp.MODE value is Sv32.
RULE 34: For SXLEN = 64 the defined paged satp.MODE values are Sv39, Sv48, and Sv57, with Sv64 reserved for future use.
RULE 35: Any satp.MODE value not defined as Bare, Sv32, Sv39, Sv48, Sv57, or Sv64 is reserved for future use and may cause other satp fields to be reinterpreted.
RULE 36: Implementations are not required to support every defined satp.MODE value.
RULE 37: If software writes satp with a MODE that the implementation does not support, the write is ignored completely and no satp fields are modified.
RULE 38: satp is considered active when the effective privilege mode is S‑mode or U‑mode.
RULE 39: The address‑translation algorithm may begin using a given satp value only while satp is active.
RULE 40: Translations that started while satp was active are not required to finish or terminate when satp becomes inactive unless an SFENCE.VMA instruction that matches the address and ASID is executed.
RULE 41: Executing an SFENCE.VMA that matches the address and ASID guarantees that updates to address‑translation data structures become visible to subsequent implicit reads.
RULE 42: Writing satp does not impose ordering constraints between page‑table updates and later address translations.
RULE 43: Writing satp does not automatically invalidate address‑translation caches.
RULE 44: When the new address space’s page tables have been modified or an ASID is reused, software may need to execute an SFENCE.VMA after, or sometimes before, writing satp.
RULE 45: Storing both the ASID and the page‑table base in satp enables them to be changed atomically on a context switch.
RULE 46: Changing satp atomically on a context switch prevents pollution of one virtual address space by another and reduces context‑switch cost.
RULE 47: Implementations must only perform implicit reads of translation‑data structures that are referenced by the current contents of satp (or by a subsequent valid V=1 entry).
RULE 48: Implementations must raise exceptions only for implicit accesses generated by instruction execution and must not raise exceptions for speculative accesses that use satp‑referenced translation data.
RULE 49: Any modification to a field of satp takes effect immediately.
RULE 50: Changing satp.MODE from Bare to any other mode or from any mode back to Bare takes effect without requiring execution of an SFENCE.VMA instruction.
RULE 51: Changing satp.ASID takes effect immediately without requiring execution of an SFENCE.VMA instruction.
RULE 52: When software recycles an ASID, it must first write satp with the new page‑table pointer and the recycled ASID before executing an SFENCE.VMA.
RULE 53: The SFENCE.VMA used after recycling an ASID must have rs1 set to x0 and rs2 set to the recycled ASID value.
RULE 54: An SFENCE.VMA may be executed while a different ASID is loaded into satp provided that the subsequent load of the recycled ASID into satp simultaneously loads the new page‑table pointer.
RULE 55: If an implementation provides no ASIDs or software always uses ASID 0, software must execute an SFENCE.VMA with rs1 set to x0 after every write to satp.
RULE 56: When no global translations have been modified, the rs2 operand of the required SFENCE.VMA after a satp write must contain zero held in a register other than x0 so that global translations are not flushed.
RULE 57: If a hart implements an address‑translation cache, the cache must be private to that hart, making the ASID field of satp local to each hart.
RULE 58: For implementations where satp.MODE is read‑only zero (always Bare), executing an SFENCE.VMA may raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 59: When the value Sv32 is written to the MODE field of the satp register, the processor selects the 32‑bit paged virtual‑memory system (Sv32).
RULE 60: When satp is configured for Sv32 mode, the supervisor translates both supervisor and user virtual addresses to supervisor physical addresses by traversing a radix‑tree page table.
RULE 61: The Sv32 mode of satp is architecturally supported only if the implementation’s SXLEN equals 32.
RULE 62: satp.MODE field selects which virtual‑memory translation scheme is used.
RULE 63: When satp.MODE is Sv32, supervisor virtual addresses are translated to supervisor physical addresses by a two‑level page‑table walk.
RULE 64: In Sv32 mode, satp causes a 20‑bit virtual‑page number to be translated into a 22‑bit physical‑page number while leaving the 12‑bit page offset unchanged.
RULE 65: The supervisor‑level physical address produced by the translation governed by satp is checked by any physical‑memory‑protection structures before it is used as a machine‑level physical address.
RULE 66: When satp.MODE equals Sv32, the resulting physical address has the implementation’s full physical‑address width and therefore is not zero‑extended.
RULE 67: When satp.MODE equals Bare, the 32‑bit virtual address is taken as a 32‑bit physical address and is zero‑extended by satp to the machine‑level physical‑address width.
RULE 68: satp holds the physical page number of the root page table used for Sv32 address translation.
RULE 69: The root page table referenced by satp must be aligned to a page boundary and occupy exactly one page (2 KiB of 4‑byte entries).
RULE 70: The physical address of the root page table is computed as satp.ppn multiplied by the system page size.
RULE 71: The root page‑table physical address computed from satp is used as the starting address for the page‑table walk in the address‑translation algorithm.
RULE 72: Address translation proceeds only when satp is active.
RULE 73: satp is defined as active when the effective privilege mode is S‑mode or U‑mode.
RULE 74: When the effective privilege mode is M‑mode, satp is not active and the address‑translation algorithm does not start.
RULE 75: Implementations may speculatively execute the address‑translation algorithm at any time provided that satp is active.
RULE 76: During speculative execution, the translation process must not set the dirty (D) bit of any page‑table entry.
RULE 77: During speculative execution, the translation process must not raise any exceptions.
RULE 78: During speculative execution, the translation process must not create translation‑cache entries that could be invalidated by an SFENCE.VMA issued after speculation begins.
RULE 79: The page‑table walker may access only those page‑table entries that are reachable from the root page table whose address is derived from satp.ppn.
RULE 80: Consequently, the range of physical addresses that the implementation may touch during the page‑table walk is entirely determined by satp and therefore under supervisor control.
RULE 81: The satp register includes a PPN field.
RULE 82: The PPN field of satp holds the physical page number of the root page table.
RULE 83: The address space used for implicit reads of NAPOT page‑table entries is the address space identified in satp as loaded by step 1 of the address‑translation algorithm.
RULE 84: If the VTVM bit in the hstatus register is set to 1 and the processor is executing in VS-mode, then any attempt to read or write the satp CSR triggers a virtual‑instruction exception.
RULE 85: The virtual‑instruction exception generated by a VS-mode access to satp when VTVM=1 is defined as part of the VTVM‑controlled virtualization of the SFENCE.VMA instruction.
RULE 86: The virtual‑instruction exception generated by a VS-mode access to satp when VTVM=1 is defined as part of the VTVM‑controlled virtualization of the SINVAL.VMA instruction.
RULE 87: The satp CSR contains the physical page number (PPN) of the root page table that is used for address translation.
RULE 88: A write to satp that specifies an unsupported MODE value is ignored and does not modify the register.
RULE 89: When the V bit in mstatus equals 1, any instruction that reads satp actually reads the contents of vsatp.
RULE 90: When the V bit in mstatus equals 1, any instruction that writes satp actually writes to vsatp instead of the physical satp register.
RULE 91: When V equals 1, a write to satp that specifies an unsupported MODE value is ignored and no update to vsatp occurs.
RULE 92: When V equals 0, accesses to satp follow the normal architectural definition and are not altered by the contents of vsatp.
RULE 93: When V equals 0, satp does not affect machine behavior except when a virtual‑machine load/store instruction or the MPRV feature in mstatus forces the operation to be performed as if V were 1.
RULE 94: The SFENCE.VMA instruction affects only memory‑management data structures that are governed by the current satp register.
RULE 95: When the virtual‑machine mode bit V equals 0, the current satp register is the HS‑level satp.
RULE 96: When the virtual‑machine mode bit V equals 1, the current satp register is the vsatp CSR, which functions as the VS‑level address‑translation register.
RULE 97: When the TVM field in mstatus is set, instruction traps are enabled that intercept accesses to the satp CSR.
RULE 98: If a satp access is intercepted by a TVM‑enabled trap, the referenced page tables may be replaced with shadow page tables that merge the OS’s translations with machine‑level translations.
RULE 99: The TVM field does not affect accesses to vsatp, and accesses to vsatp are not trapped even when TVM=1 because the TVM‑enabled trap applies only to satp.
RULE 100: During G‑stage address translation, the satp register is not consulted and the hgatp register provides the translation controls in its place.
RULE 101: No architectural mechanism allows an atomic update of the vsatp field of the satp register together with the hgatp register.
RULE 102: The recommended world‑switch sequence for the vsatp field of the satp register is to first write zero to vsatp, then write the new value to the hgatp register, and finally write the new value to vsatp.
RULE 103: When the PBMTE bit in henvcfg must be changed during a world‑switch, the required ordering is to first write zero to the vsatp field of the satp register, then modify henvcfg.PBMTE, and finally write the new value to vsatp.
RULE 104: Performing the zero‑vsatp, PBMTE‑change, new‑vsatp ordering for the satp register eliminates the need to execute an HFENCE.VVMA instruction.
RULE 105: In VS‑mode, if the VTVM field of hstatus is 1 and software attempts to read or write the satp CSR, the processor raises a virtual‑instruction exception with exception code 22.
RULE 106: The virtual‑instruction exception for a satp access is taken only when virtualization is enabled (V=1) and it replaces the illegal‑instruction exception that would otherwise be generated for the same access.
RULE 107: When a virtual‑instruction exception is taken because of a satp access, the mtval or stval register is written using the same format as for an illegal‑instruction trap.
RULE 108: If satp.MODE is Bare and the effective privilege mode is S-mode or U-mode, all shadow‑stack memory accesses are prohibited.
RULE 109: If V=1 and vsatp.MODE is Bare and the effective privilege mode is S-mode or U-mode, all shadow‑stack memory accesses are prohibited.
RULE 110: When satp.MODE is Bare and the effective privilege mode is below M, any shadow‑stack instruction that accesses memory must raise a store/AMO access‑fault exception.
RULE 111: When V=1 and vsatp.MODE is Bare and the effective privilege mode is below M, any shadow‑stack instruction that accesses memory must raise a store/AMO access‑fault exception.
RULE 112: The satp CSR contains a MODE field that selects the current address‑translation mode.
RULE 113: If the active satp register’s MODE field is Bare, the implementation applies the "ignore" transformation to physical addresses, including guest‑physical addresses.
RULE 114: If the active satp register’s MODE field is not Bare, the implementation does not apply the "ignore" transformation to physical addresses and instead handles the address as a virtual address.
RULE 115: In M-mode, accessing satp register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x180; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 116: In S-mode, accessing satp register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x180; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 117: In U-mode, accessing satp register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x180; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 118: In M-mode, setting satp.MODE (bit 60) must not be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000000000000; csrs 0x180, a0; csrr a1, 0x180; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x0].
RULE 119: In M-mode, setting satp.ASID (bit 44) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000000000; csrs 0x180, a0; csrr a1, 0x180; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000000000].
RULE 120: In M-mode, setting satp.PPN (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x180, a0; csrr a1, 0x180; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 121: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the satp register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x180, a0; csrr a1, 0x180; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 122: In M-mode, accessing satp register while Svpbmt extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Svpbmt; csrr a0, 0x180; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: scause
RULE 1: When a trap is delegated to S-mode, the scause register is written with the trap cause.
RULE 2: When a trap is delegated to S-mode, scause is written while the mcause, mepc, mtval, MPP, and MPIE registers are not written.
RULE 3: The trap handler must save the current value of the scause register when preserving state required for resuming after a trap.
RULE 4: After saving scause (together with sepc and stval), the trap handler must clear the SDT bit.
RULE 5: The interrupt cause number i reported by the scause CSR identifies bit i in the sip CSR as the pending status for that interrupt.
RULE 6: The interrupt cause number i reported by the scause CSR identifies bit i in the sie CSR as the enable status for that interrupt.
RULE 7: The scause CSR is an SXLEN‑bit read‑write register.
RULE 8: When a trap is taken into S‑mode, the implementation writes scause with a code that identifies the trap event.
RULE 9: When no trap is taken, the implementation never writes scause; it may be written only by software explicitly.
RULE 10: The most‑significant bit of scause is the interrupt bit and it is set to 1 if the trap was caused by an interrupt, otherwise it is 0.
RULE 11: The remaining bits of scause form the exception‑code field, which is a Write‑Legal‑Read‑Legal (WLRL) field.
RULE 12: The exception‑code field of scause must be able to represent values 0 through 31 (bits 4‑0).
RULE 13: Values of the exception‑code field of scause outside the defined set are permitted only for platform‑specific or custom uses.
RULE 14: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 0, the cause is reserved.
RULE 15: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 1, the cause is a supervisor software interrupt.
RULE 16: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 2, 3, or 4, the cause is reserved.
RULE 17: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 5, the cause is a supervisor timer interrupt.
RULE 18: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 6, 7, or 8, the cause is reserved.
RULE 19: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 9, the cause is a supervisor external interrupt.
RULE 20: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 10, 11, or 12, the cause is reserved.
RULE 21: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 13, the cause is a counter‑overflow interrupt.
RULE 22: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 14 or 15, the cause is reserved.
RULE 23: If scause interrupt bit is 1 and exception code is 16 or greater, the cause is designated for platform use.
RULE 24: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 0, the cause is instruction address misaligned.
RULE 25: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 1, the cause is instruction access fault.
RULE 26: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 2, the cause is illegal instruction.
RULE 27: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 3, the cause is breakpoint.
RULE 28: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 4, the cause is load address misaligned.
RULE 29: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 5, the cause is load access fault.
RULE 30: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 6, the cause is store/AMO address misaligned.
RULE 31: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 7, the cause is store/AMO access fault.
RULE 32: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 8, the cause is environment call from U‑mode.
RULE 33: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 9, the cause is environment call from S‑mode.
RULE 34: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 10 or 11, the cause is reserved.
RULE 35: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 12, the cause is instruction page fault.
RULE 36: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 13, the cause is load page fault.
RULE 37: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 14, the cause is reserved.
RULE 38: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 15, the cause is store/AMO page fault.
RULE 39: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 16 or 17, the cause is reserved.
RULE 40: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 18, the cause is software check.
RULE 41: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 19, the cause is hardware error.
RULE 42: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 20, 21, 22, or 23, the cause is reserved.
RULE 43: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 24 through 31, the cause is designated for custom use.
RULE 44: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 32 through 47, the cause is reserved.
RULE 45: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 48 through 63, the cause is designated for custom use.
RULE 46: If scause interrupt bit is 0 and exception code is 64 or greater, the cause is reserved.
RULE 47: Reads of scause must return the value most recently written to it, either by software or by the implementation when a trap occurs, even if the written exception code is illegal.
RULE 48: The encoding of scause is independent of synchronous exception priority definitions.
RULE 49: scause is a supervisor-mode register.
RULE 50: scause is a read/write register.
RULE 51: When the V bit in mstatus or sstatus is 1, any instruction that reads scause reads vscause instead, and any instruction that writes scause writes vscause instead.
RULE 52: When the V bit in mstatus or sstatus is 0, accesses to scause operate on the actual scause register and are not redirected.
RULE 53: Every value that can be held in scause must be representable in vscause.
RULE 54: When a trap is taken into S-mode, hardware writes the scause register with a cause value that identifies the trap.
RULE 55: The most-significant bit of scause is the interrupt flag and is set to 1 for an interrupt and to 0 for an exception.
RULE 56: When the hypervisor extension is implemented, the encoding of scause is identical to the encoding of mcause.
RULE 57: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 0, the interrupt cause is reserved.
RULE 58: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 1, the interrupt cause is a supervisor software interrupt.
RULE 59: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 2, the interrupt cause is a virtual supervisor software interrupt.
RULE 60: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 3, the interrupt cause is a machine software interrupt.
RULE 61: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 4, the interrupt cause is reserved.
RULE 62: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 5, the interrupt cause is a supervisor timer interrupt.
RULE 63: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 6, the interrupt cause is a virtual supervisor timer interrupt.
RULE 64: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 7, the interrupt cause is a machine timer interrupt.
RULE 65: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 8, the interrupt cause is reserved.
RULE 66: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 9, the interrupt cause is a supervisor external interrupt.
RULE 67: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 10, the interrupt cause is a virtual supervisor external interrupt.
RULE 68: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 11, the interrupt cause is a machine external interrupt.
RULE 69: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 12, the interrupt cause is a supervisor guest external interrupt.
RULE 70: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 13, the interrupt cause is reserved for counter‑overflow interrupt.
RULE 71: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 14 or 15, the interrupt cause is reserved.
RULE 72: If the interrupt flag in scause is 1 and the remaining bits are 16 or greater, the interrupt cause is designated for platform use.
RULE 73: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 0, the exception cause is instruction address misaligned.
RULE 74: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 1, the exception cause is instruction access fault.
RULE 75: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 2, the exception cause is illegal instruction.
RULE 76: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 3, the exception cause is breakpoint.
RULE 77: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 4, the exception cause is load address misaligned.
RULE 78: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 5, the exception cause is load access fault.
RULE 79: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 6, the exception cause is store/AMO address misaligned.
RULE 80: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 7, the exception cause is store/AMO access fault.
RULE 81: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 8, the exception cause is environment call from U-mode or VU-mode.
RULE 82: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 9, the exception cause is environment call from HS-mode.
RULE 83: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 10, the exception cause is environment call from VS-mode.
RULE 84: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 11, the exception cause is environment call from M-mode.
RULE 85: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 12, the exception cause is instruction page fault.
RULE 86: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 13, the exception cause is load page fault.
RULE 87: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 14, the exception cause is reserved.
RULE 88: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 15, the exception cause is store/AMO page fault.
RULE 89: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are between 16 and 19 inclusive, the exception cause is reserved.
RULE 90: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 20, the exception cause is instruction guest-page fault.
RULE 91: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 21, the exception cause is load guest-page fault.
RULE 92: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 22, the exception cause is virtual instruction.
RULE 93: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 23, the exception cause is store/AMO guest-page fault.
RULE 94: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are between 24 and 31 inclusive, the exception cause is designated for custom use.
RULE 95: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are between 32 and 47 inclusive, the exception cause is reserved.
RULE 96: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are between 48 and 63 inclusive, the exception cause is designated for custom use.
RULE 97: If the interrupt flag in scause is 0 and the remaining bits are 64 or greater, the exception cause is reserved.
RULE 98: When an instruction could raise more than one synchronous exception, the architectural priority determines which exception code is written to scause.
RULE 99: The cause code written to scause for a trap taken into S-mode follows the same set of interrupt and exception codes as defined for mcause, regardless of whether the trap originated in M-mode or S-mode.
RULE 100: When a trap is taken into HS‑mode, the hardware writes the scause CSR with the cause of the trap.
RULE 101: The hardware writes scause concurrently with sepc, stval, htval, and htinst when a trap is taken into HS‑mode.
RULE 102: In M-mode, accessing scause register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x142; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 103: In S-mode, accessing scause register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x142; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 104: In U-mode, accessing scause register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x142; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 105: In M-mode, setting scause.INT (bit 63) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000000000; csrs 0x142, a0; csrr a1, 0x142; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000].
RULE 106: In M-mode, setting scause.CODE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x142, a0; csrr a1, 0x142; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 107: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the scause register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x142, a0; csrr a1, 0x142; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 108: In M-mode, accessing scause register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x142; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: scounteren
RULE 1: scounteren is a required 32‑bit CSR that controls the availability of hardware performance‑monitoring counters to U‑mode.
RULE 2: When a scounteren bit that corresponds to a particular counter is clear, any attempt to read that counter register while executing in U‑mode raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 3: When a scounteren bit that corresponds to a particular counter is set, reads of that counter register from U‑mode are permitted.
RULE 4: Each scounteren field (CY, TM, IR, HPM n) is a single bit that, when set, enables access to the corresponding counter (cycle, time, instret, hpmcounter n) from U‑mode.
RULE 5: Each scounteren field (CY, TM, IR, HPM n) is a single bit that, when clear, causes an illegal‑instruction exception on any U‑mode read of the corresponding counter.
RULE 6: Any scounteren bit may be implemented as read‑only zero, which permanently prevents U‑mode access to the associated counter regardless of software writes.
RULE 7: All scounteren bits are WARL fields, meaning software may write any value but the hardware may read back zero.
RULE 8: The writability of a scounteren bit is independent of the corresponding bit in mcounteren; the state of mcounteren does not affect whether a scounteren bit can be written.
RULE 9: A U‑mode program can read a counter only if both the corresponding scounteren bit and the corresponding mcounteren bit are set; if either bit is clear, the read causes an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 10: The scounteren register is defined as a standard supervisor‑mode CSR.
RULE 11: The scounteren register has no corresponding virtual‑supervisor (VS) CSR.
RULE 12: When the virtual‑supervisor mode bit V is set to 1, the scounteren register retains its normal supervisor functionality and remains accessible as a supervisor CSR, not being replaced or hidden by any VS CSR.
RULE 13: Because the scounteren register lacks a VS duplicate, hypervisor software must explicitly save and restore the contents of scounteren when switching between virtual‑supervisor contexts.
RULE 14: In VU-mode, a hardware performance‑monitoring counter is readable only when its enable bit is set in both the scounteren register and the hcounteren register.
RULE 15: scounteren is a supervisor‑mode CSR whose bits can disable accesses to the corresponding counter CSRs.
RULE 16: When the virtualization mode bit V=1, any instruction that would be disabled by scounteren generates a virtual‑instruction exception with exception code 22 instead of an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 17: In virtual‑user mode, an attempt to access a non‑high‑half counter CSR raises a virtual‑instruction exception if the corresponding bit in scounteren is 0 while the same bit in mcounteren is 1.
RULE 18: In virtual‑user mode with XLEN=32, an attempt to access a high‑half counter CSR raises a virtual‑instruction exception if the corresponding bit in scounteren is 0 while the same bit in mcounteren is 1.
RULE 19: Each bit in scounteren functions as a supervisor‑level enable flag for its associated counter CSR, and a zero bit blocks access from virtual‑user mode when the machine‑level enable bit is set.
RULE 20: In M-mode, accessing scounteren register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x106; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 21: In S-mode, accessing scounteren register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x106; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 22: In U-mode, accessing scounteren register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x106; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 23: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM31 (bit 31) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000000].
RULE 24: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM30 (bit 30) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000000].
RULE 25: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM29 (bit 29) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20000000].
RULE 26: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM28 (bit 28) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000000].
RULE 27: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM27 (bit 27) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000].
RULE 28: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM26 (bit 26) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000000].
RULE 29: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM25 (bit 25) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000000].
RULE 30: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM24 (bit 24) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000].
RULE 31: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM23 (bit 23) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800000].
RULE 32: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM22 (bit 22) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400000].
RULE 33: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM21 (bit 21) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200000].
RULE 34: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM20 (bit 20) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000].
RULE 35: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM19 (bit 19) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000].
RULE 36: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM18 (bit 18) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000].
RULE 37: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM17 (bit 17) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20000].
RULE 38: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM16 (bit 16) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10000].
RULE 39: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM15 (bit 15) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000].
RULE 40: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM14 (bit 14) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4000].
RULE 41: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM13 (bit 13) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000].
RULE 42: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM12 (bit 12) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000].
RULE 43: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM11 (bit 11) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800].
RULE 44: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM10 (bit 10) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x400) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x400; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x400].
RULE 45: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM9 (bit 9) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200].
RULE 46: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM8 (bit 8) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100].
RULE 47: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM7 (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 48: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM6 (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 49: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM5 (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 50: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM4 (bit 4) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10].
RULE 51: In M-mode, setting scounteren.HPM3 (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 52: In M-mode, setting scounteren.IR (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 53: In M-mode, setting scounteren.TM (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 54: In M-mode, setting scounteren.CY (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 55: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the scounteren register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x106, a0; csrr a1, 0x106; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 56: In M-mode, accessing scounteren register while S extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: S; csrr a0, 0x106; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
