RULE 57: In M-mode, accessing scounteren register while Zicntr extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicntr; csrr a0, 0x106; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 58: In M-mode, accessing scounteren register while Zihpm extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zihpm; csrr a0, 0x106; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: scountinhibit
RULE 1: The Smcdeleg extension defines a new CSR named scountinhibit.
RULE 2: scountinhibit is a masked alias of the mcountinhibit register introduced by the Smcdeleg and Ssccfg extensions.
RULE 3: For performance counters delegated to S-mode, the bits of mcountinhibit are directly readable and writable through scountinhibit.
RULE 4: For performance counters not delegated to S-mode, reads of the corresponding bits in scountinhibit return zero.
RULE 5: For performance counters not delegated to S-mode, writes to the corresponding bits in scountinhibit have no effect.
RULE 6: If the menvcfg.CDE field is zero, any attempt to read or write scountinhibit causes an illegal instruction exception.
RULE 7: When the Supervisor Counter Delegation extension is enabled, any attempt to access scountinhibit while executing in VS-mode or VU-mode raises a virtual-instruction exception.
RULE 8: In M-mode, accessing scountinhibit register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x120; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 9: In S-mode, accessing scountinhibit register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x120; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 10: In U-mode, accessing scountinhibit register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x120; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 11: In M-mode, setting scountinhibit.WPRI (bit 32) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000000; csrs 0x120, a0; csrr a1, 0x120; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000000].
RULE 12: In M-mode, setting scountinhibit.HPM3_HPM31 (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x120, a0; csrr a1, 0x120; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 13: In M-mode, setting scountinhibit.IR (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x120, a0; csrr a1, 0x120; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 14: In M-mode, setting scountinhibit.CY (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x120, a0; csrr a1, 0x120; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 15: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the scountinhibit register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x120, a0; csrr a1, 0x120; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 16: In M-mode, accessing scountinhibit register while Smcdeleg extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Smcdeleg; csrr a0, 0x120; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 17: In M-mode, accessing scountinhibit register while Ssccfg extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Ssccfg; csrr a0, 0x120; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: sepc
RULE 1: When a trap is delegated to S-mode, the sepc register is written with the virtual address of the instruction that caused the trap.
RULE 2: A re‑entrant trap handler that needs to resume execution must save the architectural state required for resumption, including sepc.
RULE 3: The architectural state saved by a trap handler must contain sepc together with scause and stval.
RULE 4: After saving sepc, the trap handler should clear the SDT bit.
RULE 5: sepc is an SXLEN‑bit read/write CSR that can be accessed by software.
RULE 6: sepc[0] is hard‑wired to zero so that any read of sepc returns zero in bit 0 and any write to bit 0 has no effect.
RULE 7: On implementations that support only IALIGN=32, both sepc[1] and sepc[0] are always zero.
RULE 8: On implementations that can support IALIGN=16 or IALIGN=32, when IALIGN=32 the hardware masks sepc[1] on every read so that the read value of sepc[1] appears as zero.
RULE 9: The read‑time masking of sepc[1] when IALIGN=32 also applies to the implicit read of sepc performed by the SRET instruction.
RULE 10: Even when IALIGN=32 causes sepc[1] to be masked on reads, software may still write a value to sepc[1].
RULE 11: sepc implements a WARL field that must be capable of holding any valid virtual address, but it is not required to hold every possible invalid address, and an implementation may transform an illegal write value into another representable illegal value.
RULE 12: When a trap is taken into supervisor mode, the hardware writes sepc with the virtual address of the instruction that was interrupted or that caused the exception.
RULE 13: In all circumstances other than a trap to supervisor mode, the hardware never modifies sepc, so its value can change only by explicit software writes.
RULE 14: In a system that uses variable‑length instructions, when an instruction access‑fault or page‑fault exception occurs, sepc is set to the address of the first byte of the faulting instruction.
RULE 15: For an illegal‑instruction exception, if the implementation places the faulting instruction bits in stval, sepc is set to the memory address of that faulting instruction.
RULE 16: sepc is the architectural supervisor exception program counter register that holds the address to which control returns after a supervisor‑mode exception.
RULE 17: When the virtualization mode bit V equals 0, any instruction that reads sepc obtains the value from the real sepc register.
RULE 18: When V equals 0, any instruction that writes sepc stores the written value into the real sepc register.
RULE 19: When V equals 1, any instruction that reads sepc obtains the value from the virtual register vsepc instead of the real sepc register.
RULE 20: When V equals 1, any instruction that writes sepc stores the written value into the virtual register vsepc rather than the real sepc register.
RULE 21: The set of values that can be written to sepc is exactly the set of values that the WARL register vsepc is permitted to hold.
RULE 22: Therefore sepc must satisfy the same alignment and legal address‑range constraints that apply to vsepc.
RULE 23: When a trap is taken into HS-mode, the hardware writes the sepc CSR as part of the trap‑entry actions.
RULE 24: When the SRET instruction is executed in M-mode or HS-mode with the virtualization bit V set to 0, the hardware sets the program counter to the value contained in sepc.
RULE 25: The program‑counter update to sepc is performed after the status registers are updated (clearing hstatus.SP V, clearing sstatus.SPP, restoring SIE from SPIE, and setting SPIE to 1).
RULE 26: In M-mode, accessing sepc register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x141; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 27: In S-mode, accessing sepc register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x141; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 28: In U-mode, accessing sepc register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x141; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 29: In M-mode, setting sepc.PC (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x141, a0; csrr a1, 0x141; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 30: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the sepc register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x141, a0; csrr a1, 0x141; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 31: In M-mode, accessing sepc register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x141; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 32: In M-mode, accessing sepc register while S extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: S; csrr a0, 0x141; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: sstatus
RULE 1: The sstatus register contains the SUM bit.
RULE 2: The sstatus register contains the MXR bit.
RULE 3: When a cache‑block management (CMO) instruction is executed, the hardware determines whether the instruction may access a cache block based on the states of the SUM and MXR bits in sstatus together with the SUM and MXR bits in mstatus and vsstatus and the MPRV, MPV, and MPP bits in mstatus.
RULE 4: The values of the SUM and MXR bits in sstatus influence permission checks for CMO instructions in the same manner as they affect load and store instruction permissions.
RULE 5: In the sstatus register, bits [10:9] hold the shadowed copy of the vector context status field (VS) that is defined in the mstatus register.
RULE 6: The VS field in sstatus has the same definition and semantics as the VS field in mstatus, being defined analogously to the floating‑point context status field (FS).
RULE 7: The sstatus register provides a restricted view of the mstatus register in the S‑level ISA.
RULE 8: The sstatus.SDT field is a bit within the sstatus CSR that provides double‑trap control for supervisor mode.
RULE 9: When an MRET or SRET instruction is executed in M‑mode and the new privilege mode after the return is U, VS, or VU, the sstatus.SDT bit is set to 0.
RULE 10: When an MNRET instruction is executed and the new privilege mode after the return is U, VS, or VU, the sstatus.SDT bit is set to 0.
RULE 11: The sstatus CSR contains an SDT (double‑trap‑enable) bit.
RULE 12: If the Ssdbltrp extension is not implemented, the sstatus.SDT bit is read‑only zero.
RULE 13: When menvcfg.DTE = 0, the implementation must behave as if Ssdbltrp is not implemented, causing the sstatus.SDT bit to be read‑only zero.
RULE 14: The sstatus CSR includes a Floating‑point Status (FS) field and a Vector Status (VS) field.
RULE 15: When the VS field of sstatus is zero, all accesses to the vector extension’s v registers are blocked.
RULE 16: The VS field of sstatus may be initialized to zero by the machine at reset, by machine‑level software, or by the OS when it writes sstatus.
RULE 17: For RV32 implementations that implement a 64‑bit sstatus, the architecture must provide a separate sstatush CSR that holds the upper 32 bits of the full sstatus value.
RULE 18: The total number of bits available in sstatus (and in sstatush for RV32) is limited, and the specification gives no guarantee that sufficient spare bits remain to encode enable flags for all future user‑level extensions.
RULE 19: The specification does not require adding additional extension‑enable fields to sstatus; any new extension enable mechanisms must be defined outside of sstatus.
RULE 20: The RISC‑V architecture provides Supervisor‑mode Memory Access Prevention (SMAP) by using the sstatus.SUM bit.
RULE 21: The sstatus.SUM bit is a field of the sstatus CSR that controls whether a supervisor may access memory belonging to an unprivileged (user) process.
RULE 22: When the sstatus.SUM bit in sstatus is set, a supervisor is allowed to access user memory required by a specific code path.
RULE 23: When the sstatus.SUM bit in sstatus is clear, a supervisor is prohibited from accessing user memory, thereby enforcing SMAP.
RULE 24: The RISC‑V Privilege Specification defines the behavior of the sstatus register solely as described in the supplied text.
RULE 25: The sstatus CSR contains a field named SUM.
RULE 26: When the SUM bit in sstatus is set, a higher‑privileged mode may temporarily execute load and store instructions to memory regions that are normally accessible only to lower‑privileged modes such as S‑mode or U‑mode.
RULE 27: The sstatus register is a read/write CSR whose bit width equals the current SXLEN of the implementation.
RULE 28: When SXLEN=32, the bit‑field layout of the sstatus register follows the 32‑bit specification, and when SXLEN=64 it follows the 64‑bit specification.
RULE 29: On a trap entry, the sstatus.SPP field is set to 0 if the trap originated from user mode, otherwise it is set to 1.
RULE 30: When the SRET instruction is executed, the privilege level is set to user mode if sstatus.SPP=0, or to supervisor mode if sstatus.SPP=1, and afterwards sstatus.SPP is cleared to 0.
RULE 31: The sstatus.SIE field globally enables supervisor‑mode interrupts; if sstatus.SIE=0 then supervisor interrupts are not taken while the hart is in supervisor mode.
RULE 32: While the hart is executing in user mode, the value of sstatus.SIE is ignored and supervisor‑level interrupts are treated as enabled.
RULE 33: On a trap entry, sstatus.SPIE is loaded with the current value of sstatus.SIE and then sstatus.SIE is cleared to 0.
RULE 34: On execution of SRET, the sstatus.SIE field is restored from sstatus.SPIE, and then sstatus.SPIE is set to 1.
RULE 35: Every field present in the sstatus register also exists in the mstatus register with identical semantics, making sstatus a subset of mstatus.
RULE 36: Reading or writing any field of sstatus has the same effect as reading or writing the homonymous field in mstatus.
RULE 37: The sstatus register records the processor’s current operating state for supervisor mode, including privilege level and interrupt‑enable status.
RULE 38: The sstatus register contains a UXL field that selects the XLEN used for U‑mode, known as UXLEN.
RULE 39: The binary encoding of the sstatus UXL field follows the MXL encoding table, where 0b01 selects 32‑bit XLEN, 0b10 selects 64‑bit XLEN, 0b11 selects 128‑bit XLEN if the implementation supports it, and 0b00 is reserved.
RULE 40: When the XLEN for S‑mode (SXLEN) equals 32, the sstatus register does not include a UXL field and UXLEN is fixed to 32.
RULE 41: When SXLEN equals 64, the sstatus UXL field is a WARL field that records the current UXLEN, and an implementation may instead make the field read‑only, forcing UXLEN to equal SXLEN.
RULE 42: If the UXLEN recorded in sstatus differs from SXLEN, any instruction executing in the narrower mode must ignore source‑register operand bits that lie above the XLEN configured for that mode.
RULE 43: If the UXLEN recorded in sstatus differs from SXLEN, the results of instructions executed in the narrower mode must be sign‑extended to fill the widest supported XLEN in the destination register.
RULE 44: If the UXLEN recorded in sstatus is less than SXLEN, user‑mode instruction‑fetch addresses and load/store effective addresses are taken modulo 2^UXLEN.
RULE 45: When UXLEN is 32 and SXLEN is 64, user‑mode memory accesses are confined to the lowest 4 GiB of the address space as a consequence of the modulo addressing defined by sstatus.
RULE 46: When a HINT instruction encoded as an integer computational operation is executed with XLEN less than SXLEN and the upper bits of its destination register (bits SXLEN‑1 down to XLEN) are not all equal to bit XLEN‑1, the sstatus-defined behavior for those upper bits is implementation‑defined as either leaving them unchanged or overwriting them with copies of bit XLEN‑1.
RULE 47: When the sstatus MXR bit is 0, a load that accesses virtual memory succeeds only if the target page’s readable attribute (R) is 1.
RULE 48: When the sstatus MXR bit is 1, a load that accesses virtual memory succeeds if the target page’s readable (R) or executable (X) attribute is 1.
RULE 49: The sstatus MXR bit has no effect on load behavior when page‑based virtual memory translation is not active.
RULE 50: When the sstatus SUM bit is 0, any S‑mode load or store to a page marked user‑accessible (U=1) generates a fault.
RULE 51: When the sstatus SUM bit is 1, S‑mode loads and stores to pages marked user‑accessible (U=1) are permitted.
RULE 52: The sstatus SUM bit has no effect on memory accesses when page‑based virtual memory translation is not active.
RULE 53: The sstatus SUM bit has no effect on memory accesses when the processor is executing in U‑mode.
RULE 54: S‑mode execution of instructions from user‑accessible pages is prohibited regardless of the value of the sstatus SUM bit.
RULE 55: The sstatus SUM bit reads as 0 whenever satp.MODE is read‑only 0.
RULE 56: The UBE field in the sstatus register is a WARL (write‑any‑legal‑value) field.
RULE 57: The UBE field in the sstatus register determines the endianness of explicit load and store memory accesses executed in U‑mode.
RULE 58: An implementation may make the UBE field in the sstatus register read‑only and fixed to the same endianness used for S‑mode accesses.
RULE 59: When the UBE field in the sstatus register is 0, explicit memory accesses from U‑mode are performed little‑endian.
RULE 60: When the UBE field in the sstatus register is 1, explicit memory accesses from U‑mode are performed big‑endian.
RULE 61: The UBE field in the sstatus register has no effect on instruction fetches, which are always little‑endian implicit accesses.
RULE 62: For implicit accesses to supervisor‑level memory‑management data structures, S‑mode endianness applies and the UBE field in the sstatus register is ignored.
RULE 63: The sstatus register contains a field named SPELP that is defined by the Zicfilp extension.
RULE 64: When the V bit in sstatus is 0, any access to the SPELP field in sstatus is mapped to the homonymous SPELP field of the mstatus register.
RULE 65: When the V bit in sstatus is 1, any access to the SPELP field in sstatus is mapped to the homonymous SPELP field of the vsstatus register.
RULE 66: The sstatus CSR contains the SDT bit, which is a WARL (write‑any‑read‑legal) field introduced by the Ssdbltrp extension to control double‑trap handling for traps taken to privilege modes lower than M.
RULE 67: When software writes the SDT bit of sstatus to 1 using a CSR write, the hardware automatically clears the SIE bit in sstatus to 0, regardless of any value provided for SIE in the same write.
RULE 68: The SIE bit in sstatus may be set to 1 only if the SDT bit is being cleared to 0 by the same CSR write or if the SDT bit is already 0 at the moment of the write.
RULE 69: On a trap that would be taken to S‑mode, hardware reads the current SDT bit of sstatus; if SDT is 0 the hardware sets SDT to 1 in sstatus and the trap is delivered to S‑mode normally.
RULE 70: On a trap that would be taken to S‑mode, if the SDT bit of sstatus is already 1 the hardware classifies the trap as unexpected, generates a double‑trap exception with exception code 16, and delivers it to M‑mode while leaving SDT at 1.
RULE 71: Executing the SRET instruction causes the hardware to clear the SDT bit of sstatus to 0.
RULE 72: After a trap handler has saved scause, sepc, and stval and re‑enters, software is required to clear the SDT bit of sstatus (typically by executing SRET) so that a subsequent double trap can be detected during the tail phase of handling.
RULE 73: An interrupt i traps to S‑mode only if the current privilege mode is S and the SIE bit in sstatus is set, or if the current privilege mode is less privileged than S‑mode.
RULE 74: For an interrupt i to trap to S‑mode, the corresponding interrupt bits for i must be set in both sip and sie, and this decision is evaluated together with the SIE bit in sstatus.
RULE 75: Any write to the sstatus CSR must cause the processor to re‑evaluate the interrupt‑trap conditions immediately after the write completes.
RULE 76: Execution of the SRET instruction must cause the processor to re‑evaluate the interrupt‑trap conditions immediately after the instruction completes.
RULE 77: The processor must complete evaluation of interrupt‑trap conditions within a bounded amount of time after an interrupt becomes pending or ceases pending in sip, even when the pending status is influenced by the value of sstatus.
RULE 78: The bounded‑time evaluation requirement also applies after any explicit write to sstatus, because interrupt‑trap conditions expressly depend on sstatus.
RULE 79: A write to the SUM field of the sstatus register becomes architecturally effective immediately without requiring an SFENCE.VMA instruction.
RULE 80: A write to the MXR field of the sstatus register becomes architecturally effective immediately without requiring an SFENCE.VMA instruction.
RULE 81: The SUM bit is a field defined in the sstatus register.
RULE 82: When the SUM bit in sstatus is 1, supervisor‑mode software is permitted to read or write pages whose PTE U bit is 1.
RULE 83: When the SUM bit in sstatus is 0, any supervisor‑mode read or write access to a page whose PTE U bit is 1 must raise a page‑fault exception.
RULE 84: Regardless of the value of the SUM bit in sstatus, supervisor mode may never execute instructions from pages whose PTE U bit is 1.
RULE 85: sstatus is a standard supervisor status register (CSR) in the RISC‑V architecture.
RULE 86: The virtual‑supervisor CSR vsstatus is defined to duplicate the contents of sstatus.
RULE 87: When the virtual‑supervisor mode bit V is set to 1, any instruction that would normally read or modify sstatus is instead redirected to the corresponding virtual supervisor CSR vsstatus.
RULE 88: While V=1, the physical sstatus register retains its previous value and does not affect machine behavior unless a later specification explicitly states otherwise.
RULE 89: When V=0, sstatus behaves as the ordinary supervisor status register, being readable and writable by CSR instructions in the usual way.
RULE 90: When a trap is taken into HS-mode, the SPP bit in sstatus must be set to the nominal privilege mode that was active at the moment the trap was taken.
RULE 91: If the SPVP bit is not present, virtual‑machine load and store instructions (HLV, HLVX, HSV) must obtain the effective privilege of their memory accesses from the SPP bit in sstatus.
RULE 92: Interrupt i traps to HS-mode only if, when the current privilege mode is HS-mode, the SIE bit in sstatus is set.
RULE 93: When the current privilege mode is less privileged than HS-mode, the value of the SIE bit in sstatus does not affect whether interrupt i traps to HS-mode.
RULE 94: Interrupt i may trap to HS-mode only if the SIE bit in sstatus is set and the interrupt is pending and enabled in either the supervisor registers (sip and sie) or the hypervisor registers (hip and hie).
RULE 95: Interrupt i will not trap to HS-mode if its delegation bit is set in hideleg, even when the SIE bit in sstatus is set.
RULE 96: The sstatus CSR is the supervisor‑mode status register at the HS privilege level.
RULE 97: When the virtualization bit V is 1, any instruction that reads or writes sstatus actually accesses the vsstatus register instead.
RULE 98: When V is 1, the FS field of sstatus and the FS field of vsstatus are both simultaneously in effect.
RULE 99: If either the sstatus.FS field or the vsstatus.FS field is 0 (Off), executing any floating‑point instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 100: Any operation that modifies the floating‑point state while V is 1 forces both the sstatus.FS field and the vsstatus.FS field to be set to 3 (Dirty).
RULE 101: When V is 1, the VS field of sstatus and the VS field of vsstatus are both simultaneously in effect.
RULE 102: If either the sstatus.VS field or the vsstatus.VS field is 0 (Off), executing any vector instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 103: Any operation that modifies the vector state while V is 1 forces both the sstatus.VS field and the vsstatus.VS field to be set to 3 (Dirty).
RULE 104: When a hypervisor virtual‑machine load or store instruction is executed, the HS‑level sstatus.SUM bit is ignored and its value does not influence any permission checks for that memory access.
RULE 105: The sstatus.MXR bit causes explicit load instructions to be able to read pages that are marked execute‑only in both the virtual‑supervisor translation stage (VS‑stage) and the guest translation stage (G‑stage).
RULE 106: In contrast to sstatus.MXR, the vsstatus.MXR bit affects permission checks only in the first translation stage (VS‑stage) and does not apply to the second translation stage.
RULE 107: The sstatus register is defined only for the HS privilege level.
RULE 108: Every bit and field defined in the sstatus register is also present in the mstatus register.
RULE 109: Writing any bit or field of the sstatus register updates the corresponding bit or field in the mstatus register.
RULE 110: Reading any bit or field of the sstatus register yields the same value as reading the corresponding bit or field in the mstatus register.
RULE 111: The FS and VS bits in the sstatus register are exception‑generation fields that do not follow the usual HS‑qualified rule applied to other CSRs.
RULE 112: If an instruction is prevented from executing because the FS bit in the sstatus register equals 0, the processor must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 113: If an instruction is prevented from executing because the VS bit in the sstatus register equals 0, the processor must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 114: The processor must never raise a virtual‑instruction exception (cause 22) when an instruction is blocked due to FS=0 or VS=0 in the sstatus register.
RULE 115: The specification codifies the early hypervisor‑extension behavior of treating the FS and VS bits in the sstatus register in this manner to preserve software compatibility.
RULE 116: When a trap is taken into HS-mode, the hardware writes the SPIE field of sstatus.
RULE 117: When a trap is taken into HS-mode, the hardware writes the SIE field of sstatus.
RULE 118: The values written to the SPIE and SIE fields of sstatus on a trap into HS-mode are defined by the trap‑entry mechanism.
RULE 119: When a trap into HS-mode occurs from U-mode, the hardware clears the SPP field of sstatus to 0.
RULE 120: When a trap into HS-mode occurs from VU-mode, the hardware clears the SPP field of sstatus to 0.
RULE 121: When a trap into HS-mode occurs from HS-mode, the hardware sets the SPP field of sstatus to 1.
RULE 122: When a trap into HS-mode occurs from VS-mode, the hardware sets the SPP field of sstatus to 1.
RULE 123: No fields of sstatus other than SPIE, SIE, and SPP are modified when a trap is taken into HS-mode.
RULE 124: sstatus includes a field named SPP that holds the previous privilege mode bit.
RULE 125: sstatus includes a field named SIE that holds the supervisor interrupt‑enable bit.
RULE 126: sstatus includes a field named SPIE that holds the supervisor previous interrupt‑enable bit.
RULE 127: When the SRET instruction is executed with V=0 (i.e., from M‑mode or HS‑mode), the new privilege mode is determined by the values of hstatus.SP V and sstatus.SPP.
RULE 128: When the SRET instruction is executed with V=0, sstatus.SPP is cleared to 0.
RULE 129: When the SRET instruction is executed with V=0, sstatus.SIE is set to the value previously held in sstatus.SPIE.
RULE 130: When the SRET instruction is executed with V=0, sstatus.SPIE is set to 1.
RULE 131: The sstatus register provides access to the SPELP bit, which is defined in the mstatus CSR.
RULE 132: The sstatus SPELP bit stores the previous Expected Landing Pad (ELP) state when a trap is delivered to S-mode or HS-mode.
RULE 133: When a trap is taken to S-mode, the sstatus S-PELP field is written with the current ELP value.
RULE 134: After the sstatus S-PELP field is written on trap entry to S-mode, the global ELP field is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED.
RULE 135: During execution of an SRET instruction, if the new privilege mode has LPE = 1, the ELP is restored from the sstatus S-PELP field.
RULE 136: During execution of an SRET instruction, if the new privilege mode does not have LPE = 1, the ELP is set to NO_LP_EXPECTED.
RULE 137: After the SRET‑induced update, the sstatus S-PELP field is cleared to NO_LP_EXPECTED.
RULE 138: The S-mode trap handler must save the sstatus S-PELP bit and the x7 register before performing an indirect call or jump when sstatus LPE = 1.
RULE 139: If S-mode can respond to interrupts and sstatus LPE = 1, the handler should also save the sstatus S-PELP bit before re‑enabling interrupts.
RULE 140: The S-PELP bit is also defined in the vsstatus CSR for VS-mode, mirroring its definition in the sstatus register.
RULE 141: If the Ssdbltrp extension is implemented, the sstatus CSR contains a field named SDT.
RULE 142: The SDT field is part of the sstatus register when the Ssdbltrp extension is present.
RULE 143: In M-mode, accessing sstatus register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x100; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 144: In S-mode, accessing sstatus register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x100; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 145: In U-mode, accessing sstatus register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x100; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 146: In M-mode, setting sstatus.SD (bit 63) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000000000; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000].
RULE 147: In M-mode, setting sstatus.UXL (bit 32) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100000000; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100000000].
RULE 148: In M-mode, setting sstatus.MXR (bit 19) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80000; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80000].
RULE 149: In M-mode, setting sstatus.SUM (bit 18) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40000; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40000].
RULE 150: In M-mode, setting sstatus.XS (bit 15) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000].
RULE 151: In M-mode, setting sstatus.FS (bit 13) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2000; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2000].
RULE 152: In M-mode, setting sstatus.VS (bit 9) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x200) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x200; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x200].
RULE 153: In M-mode, setting sstatus.SPP (bit 8) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x100) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x100; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x100].
RULE 154: In M-mode, setting sstatus.UBE (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 155: In M-mode, setting sstatus.SPIE (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 156: In M-mode, setting sstatus.SIE (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 157: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the sstatus register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x100, a0; csrr a1, 0x100; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 158: In M-mode, accessing sstatus register while Ssdbltrp extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Ssdbltrp; csrr a0, 0x100; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 159: In M-mode, accessing sstatus register while Zicfilp extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicfilp; csrr a0, 0x100; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: stvec
RULE 1: When a double trap originates in VS-mode, after M‑mode redirects the exception to HS‑mode, the following MRET instruction must resume execution at the address contained in the stvec register.
RULE 2: The stvec register is a SXLEN‑bit read/write CSR that holds the supervisor trap‑vector configuration.
RULE 3: The stvec register contains a BASE field that stores bits XLEN‑1 through 2 of a virtual or physical address, with the lower two bits implicitly zero when the address is used.
RULE 4: The address represented by the stvec BASE field is always 4‑byte aligned.
RULE 5: The stvec BASE field may hold any valid virtual or physical address provided it satisfies the required alignment constraints.
RULE 6: The stvec BASE field must be at least 4‑byte aligned regardless of the MODE setting.
RULE 7: When the stvec MODE is set to a value other than Direct, additional alignment constraints may be imposed on the BASE field.
RULE 8: The stvec register includes a MODE field that encodes the trap‑vector mode.
RULE 9: A stvec MODE value of 0 selects Direct mode, in which all traps set the program counter to the BASE address.
RULE 10: A stvec MODE value of 1 selects Vectored mode, in which synchronous exceptions set the program counter to the BASE address.
RULE 11: In Vectored mode, an asynchronous interrupt sets the program counter to BASE plus four times the interrupt cause number.
RULE 12: In Vectored mode, an interrupt with cause 0x5 causes the program counter to be set to BASE plus 0x14.
RULE 13: Any stvec MODE value of 2 or greater is reserved and must not be used for normal operation.
RULE 14: Writing to the stvec register updates both the BASE and MODE fields according to the written value.
RULE 15: Reading the stvec register returns the stored BASE bits (XLEN‑1 through 2) and the MODE bits as defined by the CSR encoding.
RULE 16: stvec is a supervisor‑mode register that holds the supervisor trap‑vector base address and the trap‑mode selector.
RULE 17: stvec is a read/write architectural register, and any instruction that reads or writes stvec accesses this register directly.
RULE 18: In stvec, bits XLEN‑1 down to 2 constitute the BASE field, which is WARL (any value may be written and any written value is read back as a legal value).
RULE 19: In stvec, bits 1‑0 constitute the MODE field, which is also WARL (any value may be written and any written value is read back as a legal value).
RULE 20: When the virtualization enable bit V is 1, accesses that would normally target stvec are redirected to the virtual supervisor register vstvec instead.
RULE 21: When the virtualization enable bit V is 0, vstvec has no effect and all accesses to the trap‑vector register affect the real stvec register.
RULE 22: Software may write either a tagged or an untagged address into the stvec CSR, and the write is stored without any pointer‑masking transformation.
RULE 23: When a trap is taken, the hardware reads the trap‑handler address from stvec and transfers control to that address without applying pointer masking.
RULE 24: Pointer masking is applied only to hardware‑generated CSR writes and never to stvec, because stvec is never written by hardware.
RULE 25: In M-mode, accessing stvec register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x105; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 26: In S-mode, accessing stvec register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x105; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 27: In U-mode, accessing stvec register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x105; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 28: In M-mode, setting stvec.BASE (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x105, a0; csrr a1, 0x105; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 29: In M-mode, setting stvec.MODE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x105, a0; csrr a1, 0x105; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 30: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the stvec register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x105, a0; csrr a1, 0x105; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 31: In M-mode, accessing stvec register while H extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: H; csrr a0, 0x105; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 32: In M-mode, accessing stvec register while M extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: M; csrr a0, 0x105; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 33: In M-mode, accessing stvec register while S extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: S; csrr a0, 0x105; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 34: In M-mode, accessing stvec register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x105; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: tdata1
RULE 1: The tdata1.type field value 6 identifies a Type‑6 trigger.
RULE 2: When tdata1.type equals 6 and mcontrol6.select equals 0, the trigger is an address‑match trigger and the specification recommends that it be supported.
RULE 3: When tdata1.type equals 6 and mcontrol6.select equals 1, the trigger is a data‑match trigger.
RULE 4: When tdata1.type equals 6, mcontrol6.select equals 1, and the Zicbom extension is implemented, the specification recommends that the trigger be unsupported.
RULE 5: When tdata1.type equals 6, mcontrol6.select equals 1, and the Zicboz extension is implemented, the specification recommends that the trigger be supported (implementation is optional).
RULE 6: When tdata1.type equals 6, mcontrol6.select equals 1, and the Zicbop extension is implemented, the specification recommends that the trigger be unsupported.
RULE 7: The tdata1.type field value 2 identifies a Type‑2 trigger that provides address/data‑match functionality and the specification recommends that it be unsupported.
RULE 8: In M-mode, accessing tdata1 register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x7a1; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 9: In S-mode, accessing tdata1 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x7a1; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 10: In U-mode, accessing tdata1 register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x7a1; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 11: In M-mode, setting tdata1.TYPE (bit 60) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1000000000000000; csrs 0x7a1, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a1; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1000000000000000].
RULE 12: In M-mode, setting tdata1.DMODE (bit 59) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x800000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x800000000000000; csrs 0x7a1, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a1; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x800000000000000].
RULE 13: In M-mode, setting tdata1.DATA (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x7a1, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a1; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 14: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the tdata1 register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x7a1, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a1; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 15: In M-mode, accessing tdata1 register while Zicbom extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicbom; csrr a0, 0x7a1; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 16: In M-mode, accessing tdata1 register while Zicbop extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicbop; csrr a0, 0x7a1; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 17: In M-mode, accessing tdata1 register while Zicboz extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zicboz; csrr a0, 0x7a1; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: tselect
RULE 1: The values written to the tselect register reside in an address space that is distinct from the CSR number address space.
RULE 2: The values written to the tselect register reside in an address space that is distinct from the tselect values defined by the Sdtrig extension.
RULE 3: In M-mode, accessing tselect register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x7a0; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 4: In S-mode, accessing tselect register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x7a0; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 5: In U-mode, accessing tselect register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x7a0; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 6: In M-mode, setting tselect.INDEX (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x7a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a0; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 7: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the tselect register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x7a0, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a0; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 8: In M-mode, accessing tselect register while Sdtrig extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Sdtrig; csrr a0, 0x7a0; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: mcontext
RULE 1: In M-mode, accessing mcontext register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x7a8; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 2: In S-mode, accessing mcontext register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x7a8; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 3: In U-mode, accessing mcontext register must result in illegal-instruction exception (# Result: mcause = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x7a8; # Result: mcause = 0x2].
RULE 4: In M-mode, setting mcontext.HCONTEXT (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x7a8, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a8; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 5: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the mcontext register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x7a8, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a8; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].

REGISTER: tcontrol
RULE 1: In M-mode, accessing tcontrol register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x7a5; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 2: In S-mode, accessing tcontrol register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x7a5; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 3: In U-mode, accessing tcontrol register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x7a5; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 4: In M-mode, setting tcontrol.WPRI (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x7a5, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a5; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 5: In M-mode, setting tcontrol.MTE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x7a5, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a5; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 6: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the tcontrol register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x7a5, a0; csrr a1, 0x7a5; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
